state_snapshot.user_query: Assess the likely AIE mechanism for this molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal torsion snapshots across two independent aryl–vinyl dihedrals show repeatable twist-induced oscillator-strength collapse with state-ordering change at 90° (S1 becomes nearly dark; first bright shifts to S2), and S1 is not energetically stabilized upon twisting. Verifier close-family precedent for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens assigns emission to an ICT (single CT band) state with RIR/AIE, explicitly noting absence of TICT hallmarks and S1 minima at moderate twist (not ~90°).

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Twisting produces a very weakly allowed lowest state at 90° (dark S1) consistent with a twisted/decoupled CT-like configuration, but both internal torsion energetics (no stabilization of dark S1 with twist) and verifier precedent/hallmarks (no dual emission; no stabilized ~90° S1 minimum; methoxy donor typically insufficient for classic TICT) argue against TICT as the dominant emissive mechanism.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Earlier vertical/conformer evidence had bright S1 and weak conformer sensitivity, but controlled torsion forcing across two dihedrals robustly generates darkening and state-ordering changes, making a purely LE/neutral-aromatic dominant mechanism unlikely.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Direct CT-defining descriptors (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT/difference density) remain unavailable in the internal tool outputs, leaving some residual uncertainty about the precise CT/LE mixing and the role of relaxed excited-state minima.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton-transfer-capable donor/acceptor pair exists in the molecule and no evidence suggests ESIPT.

state_snapshot.verifier_supplement_summary: No exact-identity hit. Close-family push–pull cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens with alkoxy/methoxy donors and cyano acceptors are assigned ICT-AIE (single CT band; positive solvatochromism; RIR; no dual emission). Classic TICT hallmarks (dual emission; viscosity-dependent CT band; stabilized S1 minimum near ~90° twist) are emphasized as discriminators and are reported absent for close-family D–A triarylethylene/cyano-stilbene systems; computational studies place S1 minima at moderate twist, not 90°.
state_snapshot.closure_justification_summary: Internal: across two aryl–vinyl torsions, twisting causes oscillator-strength collapse and at 90° the lowest state becomes dark with brightness shifting to a higher state index, while the dark S1 is not energetically stabilized with twist. External: close-family literature assigns ICT-AIE (single CT band; no TICT dual emission; S1 minima at moderate twist) and provides mechanistic hallmarks that disfavor stabilized TICT minima for methoxy–cyano systems. Together these discriminate ICT over TICT sufficiently for closure.
state_snapshot.pairwise_task_rationale: Parsed per-angle state tables for the second torsion coordinate showed darkening with twist but no energetic stabilization of the dark S1, supporting ICT over TICT; combined with the targeted ICT-vs-TICT verifier supplement, this completes closure.

planner_diagnosis_history: Task: assess the most likely AIE mechanism (ICT vs TICT vs ESIPT vs neutral-aromatic/RIM) for the given molecule (C22H17NO; triaryl substituted alkene with one methoxy donor and one nitrile acceptor). Current top1 is TICT (0.46) with runner-up neutral aromatic (0.28). TICT is a working hypothesis because the structure has moderate planarity but several rotatable bonds and a donor–acceptor axis that could enable a twisted CT minimum; however, the provided planarity proxy is high (~0.90), which could indicate a more rigid, near-planar conjugated system where emission changes in aggregates arise more from generic restriction of vibrations/rotations (neutral-aromatic/RIM) than from accessing a distinct TICT state. Evidence to separate TICT from neutral-aromatic/RIM: (i) whether S1 has strong CT character (dipole change, hole–electron separation) and whether twisting along a key dihedral is energetically favored in S1; (ii) whether a low-oscillator-strength CT/TICT state lies below a brighter LE-like state; (iii) whether S1 geometry relaxes toward a more twisted donor/aryl arrangement. Shared prepared structure context is available (neutral singlet; 10 conformers; selected conformer id 2; 4 rotatable bonds; prepared XYZ/SDF paths provided), enabling immediate low-cost baseline quantum calculations. Macro can provide structure-based mechanistic priors and identify likely torsional coordinates and D–A partitions but cannot confirm state ordering or CT character quantitatively. Microscopic can run a single bounded baseline S0 optimization plus S1 vertical excitation to extract excitation energy, oscillator strength, and qualitative CT indicators; it cannot in round-1 do conformer/torsion scans or multi-step excited-state relaxations. First round should gather both: macro to map plausible motion/CT pathways and microscopic baseline to anchor the hypothesis with electronic-structure observables.

Round budget: 1/20 (20 remaining). Latest evidence adds (i) macro proxies: D–π–A motif with 4 rotatable bonds but high planarity_proxy≈0.899, and (ii) microscopic baseline: lowest vertical excitation is bright (S1 at 3.6776 eV, f=0.2876; pattern=lowest_state_is_bright) with HOMO–LUMO gap ~3.17 eV and sizable S0 dipole magnitude (~8.17 D). This weakens the prior Top-1 (TICT) relative to alternatives because a hallmark risk for TICT is formation of a dark twisted CT state; instead the lowest vertical state is already strongly allowed. It strengthens ICT and also keeps neutral-aromatic/LE competitive because high planarity and strong oscillator strength can indicate an LE-dominated transition. Current Top-1 is ICT (0.34) with Top-2 neutral aromatic (0.31); TICT drops to third (0.27). Key remaining discriminator between ICT vs neutral aromatic: whether S1 shows significant charge-transfer character (hole–electron separation / transferred charge / dipole change) versus being spatially overlapping LE on the conjugated triaryl-alkenyl core. Verifier supplementation remains missing; closure justification is still missing but can be advanced internally via a targeted CT-descriptor extraction from the already-generated baseline bundle. Therefore the best next action is a single bounded microscopic task to extract CT descriptors for the key low-lying bright state(s), directly discriminating ICT vs neutral aromatic (and secondarily TICT).

Round budget: 2/20 (19 rounds remaining). Current top1=neutral aromatic (0.35), top2=ICT (0.33). Latest evidence does not decisively strengthen either side: (i) macro still indicates a planar, conjugated triaryl-alkenyl scaffold with D–π–A substitution and 4 rotatable bonds; (ii) microscopic CT-descriptor extraction from the existing baseline bundle returned only partial surrogates and explicitly lacked the key CT metrics (hole–electron separation/overlap, qCT). This weakens the closure effort because the planned discriminative observable was not obtained. The remaining key discriminator between neutral aromatic vs ICT is still quantitative excited-state CT character for S1 (and possibly S2): presence/absence of significant hole–electron separation and transferred charge. Verifier supplementation remains missing; closure justification is still collecting (the targeted task ran but did not deliver the decisive descriptors). Best next action is a single bounded microscopic calculation that directly computes state character/CT diagnostics (rather than parse-only extraction), targeting states 1–2.

Round 3/20 (18 rounds remaining). Top1=neutral aromatic (0.38) vs Top2=ICT (0.30). Latest evidence weakly strengthens neutral aromatic relative to ICT: the targeted state-characterization run again failed to produce explicit CT observables (NTO/attachment–detachment/hole–electron separation/qCT all missing) and only returned dominant transitions and state-family overlap. The available proxy indicates S1 and S2 are in the same excitation family (shared occupied orbitals 81/82 and same primary virtual orbital 83; dominant transition signature reported as 82→83), which is more suggestive of a consistent delocalized π–π* manifold than a strongly state-specific CT signature. ICT remains a close challenger because the molecular topology is push–pull and the S0 dipole is large (~8.17 D), but without quantitative CT diagnostics we cannot distinguish 'delocalized LE with large permanent dipole' from genuine ICT. Key remaining discriminator (neutral aromatic vs ICT): spatial localization of frontier orbitals (HOMO vs LUMO) and/or quantitative CT metrics (charge separation/qCT), which are still unavailable. Verifier supplementation remains missing. Closure justification status remains collecting (the targeted task executed but did not yield the discriminative CT metrics). Next best action is another single bounded microscopic task that changes the observable acquisition route: compute localized-orbital / frontier-orbital localization descriptors to directly test donor-localized HOMO vs acceptor-localized LUMO (ICT) versus broadly delocalized frontier orbitals (neutral aromatic).

Round 4/20 with 17 rounds remaining. Top1=neutral aromatic (0.34), Top2=ICT (0.28). Latest evidence (microscopic) weakens closure progress rather than strengthening either hypothesis: the planned discriminative microscopic action `run_targeted_localized_orbital_analysis` failed (Amesp normal_termination_missing; stdout: 'Stop : error keyword: "lmo" in >ope!'), so no new orbital-localization/fragment-partition evidence was produced. This leaves the neutral-aromatic vs ICT question unresolved: the key remaining discriminator is whether frontier orbitals/excitation density show donor→acceptor separation (supporting ICT) versus delocalized π-density (supporting neutral aromatic). The latest failure increases residual uncertainty (unknown upweighted) and indicates an internal route blockage for the intended closure task. Verifier supplementation is still missing, and closure justification remains collecting (internal targeted tasks have not produced the decisive CT/orbital metrics). Given the internal tool-path friction and the still-missing external check, the best next action is Verifier to obtain literature/precedent discriminators for closely related cyano-stilbene / triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens and any known ICT vs LE signatures (e.g., solvatochromism, Stokes shift, polarity dependence).

Round budget: 5/20 (16 rounds remaining). After incorporating verifier supplementation (status=sufficient, evidence specificity=close_family), the top hypotheses are now top1=ICT (0.45) and top2=neutral aromatic (0.27). The verifier evidence is genuinely new and high-information relative to our previously blocked internal CT/orbital routes: multiple close-family sources/reviews report that methoxy-donor + cyano-acceptor cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens commonly show ICT character (positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts, large dipole change), while neutral-aromatic/LE behavior with negligible solvatochromism is emphasized for symmetric triarylethylenes lacking push–pull substitution. This challenges the previous top1 (neutral aromatic) more than it supports it, although it is not an exact-identity hit and therefore cannot fully close the case alone. Closure justification is not yet sufficient because we still lack a molecule-specific quantitative CT diagnostic from internal calculations (prior attempts returned proxy-only or failed with an LMO keyword/runtime issue). Next step should be a different bounded internal discriminator that avoids the stalled LMO route (e.g., density/population or charge-difference analysis for S0 vs S1/S2).

Round 6/20 (15 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.42) vs Top2=neutral aromatic (0.29). Latest microscopic follow-up (run_targeted_density_population_analysis, state_window=[1,2]) completed but was effectively proxy-only for the ICT-vs-LE question: it confirms the same bright low-lying vertical states (S1 3.6776 eV, f~0.287; S2 4.5055 eV, f~0.335; dominant signatures still centered on 82→83) and a large ground-state dipole (~8.17 D), but did not yield an interpretable donor-vs-acceptor charge redistribution (no fragment charge deltas, no qCT/hole–electron/NTO). Therefore: (i) ICT remains favored mainly by sufficient/high-gain external close-family precedent (solvatochromism-based ICT assignments for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl analogs), but is slightly weakened by the continued absence of a molecule-specific CT proxy; (ii) neutral aromatic is slightly strengthened as the main internal-model competitor because the internal evidence remains consistent with a delocalized LE-like manifold and the CT discriminator is still missing. Key remaining discriminator between ICT and neutral aromatic is a molecule-specific CT measure (e.g., transition-density spatial separation / attachment–detachment / fragment charge shift) or experimental solvatochromism magnitude for this exact compound. Verifier supplementation is sufficient; closure justification remains collecting (internal discriminator still missing). Given verifier is already sufficient, the best next step is a single bounded microscopic action that targets an alternative, registry-supported CT proxy not reliant on the previously failing LMO/NTO routes.

Round budget: 7/20 (14 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.40) vs Top2=neutral aromatic (0.30). Latest evidence (microscopic targeted transition-dipole analysis on states 1–2) adds: (i) large S0 dipole magnitude (~8.1725 D), (ii) two bright low-lying transitions with sizable transition-dipole magnitudes (S0→S1 dip≈4.347; S0→S2 dip≈3.443) and oscillator strengths ~0.315 and ~0.294, and (iii) dominant transition structure (mainly 58→59 and related contributions). This strengthens the picture of an allowed, delocalized low-lying excitation but does not provide a charge-separation metric; therefore it does not decisively support ICT over neutral-aromatic. Net effect: ICT remains top1 mainly due to sufficient close-family verifier precedent; neutral-aromatic remains a close challenger because molecule-specific CT proxies are still missing. Key remaining discriminator ICT vs neutral-aromatic: a molecule-specific CT indicator for the emitting state (e.g., excited-state vs ground-state fragment charge shift, or NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT), or experimental solvatochromism magnitude for this exact compound. Verifier supplementation is sufficient; closure justification is still collecting because internal molecule-specific CT proxy remains absent. Next action should therefore generate an internal donor→acceptor charge-redistribution proxy (excited-state charge analysis) rather than more transition-dipole-only observables.

Round 8/20 (13 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.38) vs Top2=neutral aromatic (0.30). Latest evidence (microscopic run_targeted_charge_analysis) does not strengthen ICT: only Hirshfeld charges were reported (Mulliken missing) and the returned charge table is not an interpretable excited-state CT proxy (no state-resolved delta charges or fragment charge shift). This weakens/does not advance the molecule-specific ICT closure case and increases residual uncertainty (unknown up). Neutral-aromatic remains a strong challenger because internal proxies (high planarity; bright S1; shared 82→83 excitation family) continue to be consistent with LE-like behavior. Key remaining discriminator for ICT vs neutral aromatic is still a molecule-specific excited-state charge-redistribution / CT-separation metric (NTO/hole–electron separation qCT/D_CT) or, experimentally, strong positive solvatochromism (>~30 nm) vs negligible (<~10 nm). Verifier supplementation is sufficient (close-family, high information gain, mixed but overall ICT-tilting). Closure justification remains collecting because internal evidence still lacks a decisive CT metric. Next action is best directed at an alternative in-registry microscopic route that may actually produce state-character/CT descriptors (rather than repeating charge tables that are not diagnostic).

Round 9/20 with 12 rounds remaining. Top1=ICT (0.36) vs Top2=neutral aromatic (0.31). Latest evidence (microscopic run_ris_state_characterization) is mixed/low-discriminative for the pair: it confirms large S0 dipole (~8.17 D) and bright S1/S2 with dominant transition family 82→83, but does not produce the requested CT-defining observables (NTO/attachment–detachment/hole–electron separation/qCT). This leaves ICT still primarily supported by external close-family solvatochromism precedent, while the neutral-aromatic challenger remains viable due to planar/bright LE-compatible internal patterns. Key remaining discriminator is still a molecule-specific CT metric (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT) or an experimentally motivated proxy (solvatochromism magnitude). Verifier supplementation is sufficient (close-family, high information gain, mixed relation). Closure justification remains collecting because internal molecule-specific CT metrics are still missing. Best next action is a single microscopic action that most directly targets CT state descriptors via a different in-registry route (natural orbital analysis).

Round 10/20 (11 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.34) vs Top2=neutral aromatic (0.30). Latest evidence weakens internal closure rather than strengthening either hypothesis: the targeted natural-orbital/NTO route failed at runtime with backend error keyword "natorb", mirroring the earlier localized-orbital ("lmo") failure and reinforcing that molecule-specific CT descriptors are currently blocked by tooling. ICT remains top because verifier supplementation is already sufficient and strongly favors ICT for this push–pull methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl family (solvatochromism/Stokes-shift criteria), but the lack of molecule-specific CT metrics keeps neutral-aromatic competitive. The key remaining discriminator between ICT and neutral-aromatic is still a molecule-specific CT metric (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT, or reliable donor→acceptor charge redistribution) or, experimentally, strong positive solvatochromism (>~30 nm) vs negligible solvatochromism (<~10 nm). Verifier supplementation: sufficient. Closure justification: collecting but effectively constrained by internal descriptor-generation failures. Best next action: inspect existing raw artifact bundles to see whether any hidden/parsable state-character outputs (charges, transition-density proxies, attachment/detachment-like records) exist, before spending more rounds on follow-up capabilities that trigger backend keyword failures.

Round 11/20 (10 rounds remaining). Current top1=ICT (0.33) vs top2=neutral aromatic (0.30). Latest evidence (microscopic inspect_raw_artifact_bundle on round_01_baseline_bundle) weakens internal support for ICT relative to neutral aromatic by confirming that the baseline bundle lacks essentially all molecule-specific CT discriminators: no NTO/natorb outputs, no attachment–detachment, no transition-density/difference-density files, and no per-state charge dumps. What is present is limited to vertical energies/f-values, dominant transitions, S0 dipole, MO files, and logs—insufficient to decisively label S1 as CT vs LE. This leaves the external close-family solvatochromism precedent as the main reason ICT remains top1, while neutral aromatic stays highly competitive because internal outputs are consistent with an LE-like manifold and cannot be disproven. Key remaining discriminator for ICT vs neutral aromatic: a molecule-specific CT metric (hole–electron separation/qCT/difference density) OR a robust surrogate such as consistent darkening/character change across conformers indicative of CT stabilization. Verifier supplementation remains sufficient (close-family + discriminator thresholds), but closure justification is still collecting because internal molecule-specific CT evidence is missing and now confirmed absent in baseline artifacts. Best next action is to generate a richer internal dataset via a different bounded calculation route (multi-conformer vertical excitations) to look for state-ordering/brightness patterns across conformers and potentially produce additional parsable artifacts.

Round-budget status: 12/20 (9 rounds remaining). Top1=neutral aromatic (0.34) vs Top2=ICT (0.33). Latest evidence (microscopic run_conformer_bundle; 5 conformers; states 1–3) strengthens the neutral-aromatic/LE challenger because across all sampled conformers the lowest vertical state remains bright (no emergence of a low-lying dark state), and the excitation energies vary only weakly (excitation_spread_ev≈0.0286; bright_state_sensitivity≈0.0362). This pattern is more typical of a robust delocalized π–π* (LE-like) manifold than a strongly geometry-sensitive CT state. ICT remains highly plausible due to sufficient verifier close-family precedent for this asymmetric methoxy–cyano push–pull scaffold, but is still missing molecule-specific CT diagnostics. Key remaining discriminator (ICT vs neutral aromatic): direct CT character metrics (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT or difference densities) or experimental solvatochromism magnitude for the exact compound. Verifier supplementation: sufficient (close-family), high information gain, mixed relation. Closure justification: still collecting; internal evidence is improving via surrogate discriminator (conformer robustness) but not yet decisive. Next action is best because it extracts geometry/planarity/torsion descriptors from the new conformer bundle to test whether substantial torsional variation exists and whether excitation brightness/ordering is invariant to twisting—an internal, low-cost, pairwise-relevant surrogate discriminator that does not rely on the failing LMO/NATORB/NTO routes.

Round 13/20 (8 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.35) vs Top2=neutral aromatic (0.32). Latest evidence weakens the internal closure trajectory rather than strengthening neutral-aromatic: the parse-only `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` on `round_12_conformer_bundle` returned only geometry-source and (empty) H-bond candidate fields, and failed to provide planarity/torsion/dihedral/rotor metrics or min/median/max summaries. This leaves unresolved the key discriminator between ICT and neutral-aromatic: whether the conformer-bundle’s bright-S1 robustness holds across substantively twisted geometries (strongly favoring LE) or merely across uniformly planar conformers (less discriminative). Because internal geometry-variation quantification failed, the balance tilts slightly back toward ICT given the strong close-family external solvatochromism/ICT precedent. Verifier supplementation remains sufficient (close-family; mixed but ICT-leaning). Closure justification is still collecting and remains blocked on obtaining a molecule-specific CT metric or a validated surrogate tied to torsional variation. Best next step: inspect raw conformer-bundle artifacts to confirm what geometry files exist (e.g., per-conformer S0 optimized XYZ) and whether torsion/planarity can be recovered via a different parse route or a subsequent bounded microscopic action.

Round 14/20 (7 rounds remaining). Current top1=ICT (0.34) vs top2=neutral aromatic (0.33). Latest evidence (microscopic inspect_raw_artifact_bundle on round_12_conformer_bundle) is low-discriminative for ICT vs neutral aromatic: it confirms the conformer bundle’s raw artifacts mostly comprise prepared geometry paths plus aTB/TD outputs (AIP/AOP/MO/stdout) and do not expose explicit dihedral/torsion tables or per-conformer torsion metrics in a parser-friendly way. This leaves the key surrogate argument ("bright S1 is robust even across meaningfully twisted conformers") still under-justified because torsional spread cannot be quantified. Consequently: (i) ICT is not strengthened by new molecule-specific CT observables (still missing), but retains support from sufficient close-family solvatochromism-based precedent; (ii) neutral aromatic remains strongly competitive due to conformer-robust bright lowest-state behavior, but is not decisively advantaged without demonstrated torsional diversity. Key remaining discriminator for ICT vs neutral aromatic: whether twisting (forced torsion) induces CT-like/dark-state behavior or strong state-ordering sensitivity (supporting ICT/TICT-family) versus persistent bright/LE-like lowest-state behavior even at large twists (supporting neutral aromatic/LE). Verifier supplementation remains sufficient; closure justification remains collecting because internal, molecule-specific CT descriptors are unavailable and surrogate geometry-variation quantification is blocked. Best next action is a bounded torsion-snapshot calculation that deliberately enforces twist angles on a high-relevance aryl–vinyl torsion and checks state ordering/brightness changes, which directly targets ICT vs neutral aromatic without requiring unavailable NTO/qCT tooling.

Round budget: 15/20 (6 rounds remaining). Current top1=ICT (0.42) vs top2=neutral aromatic (0.25); third=TICT (0.18). Latest evidence (torsion snapshots, 0/30/60/90° on an auto-selected high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral) strengthens ICT-family interpretations and challenges a purely neutral-aromatic/LE picture: the lowest excitation remains nominally the lowest bright state at small twists (f≈0.10 at 0–30°) but becomes effectively dark at 90° (f≈0.0006) with an excitation-energy increase (~4.40→4.66 eV) and substantial oscillator-spread (~0.10). This ‘bright-to-dark upon decoupling’ is a key CT/twist-sensitivity hallmark (supporting ICT/TICT-family) and is less consistent with an LE/neutral-aromatic state remaining allowed across torsion. The remaining discriminator between ICT vs TICT is whether emission is dominated by a planar/partially planar ICT state (ICT) versus a relaxed twisted CT minimum (TICT), which cannot be established without excited-state relaxation or robust state-character descriptors. Verifier supplementation remains sufficient (close-family solvatochromism-based ICT precedent), while closure justification is still collecting because the torsion run did not fully return the requested state window (only 2 states reported) and lacks state-character metrics.

Round 16/20 (5 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.45) and top2=TICT (0.23); neutral aromatic drops to third (0.18). Latest microscopic evidence (parsed torsion snapshot outputs for 0/30/60/90°) strengthens the CT/twist-sensitivity picture: at 90° the lowest state becomes essentially dark (S1 f=0.0006) and the first bright state shifts to S2 (4.921 eV, f=0.152), whereas at 0–60° the lowest state remains bright (S1 f≈0.103→0.087). This behavior challenges a torsion-robust neutral-aromatic LE assignment and instead supports ICT/TICT-family mechanisms. The key remaining discriminator is ICT vs TICT: does emission originate from a (partially) planar CT-mixed bright state (ICT) or from a relaxed twisted CT minimum (TICT). Verifier supplementation is sufficient for the earlier ICT-vs-neutral-aromatic question but is not specific for ICT-vs-TICT here; closure justification for ICT-vs-TICT is still collecting. The best next action is to run a second controlled torsion snapshot on a different high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral to see whether any twist coordinate stabilizes a low-lying dark state (more TICT-like) or mainly causes decoupling/brightness loss without stabilization (more ICT-like).

Round 17/20 (rounds remaining incl. current: 4). Top1=ICT (0.52) vs Top2=TICT (0.20); additional candidates: neutral aromatic (0.13), unknown (0.13), ESIPT (0.02).

Latest evidence (microscopic run_torsion_snapshots on a DIFFERENT high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral, dihedral_id=dih_4_5_6_7) strengthens the CT/twist-decoupling picture and thereby strengthens top1 ICT relative to top2 TICT: across angle offsets 0→30→60→90°, the S1 oscillator strength collapses (f: 0.3302 → 0.2966 → 0.1557 → 0.0025) and at 90° the manifold is explicitly "lowest_state_is_dark_then_bright" with the first bright state being S2 (E≈5.4288 eV, f≈0.2018). This repeats the key qualitative behavior previously seen for the first torsion coordinate: twist drives the lowest state dark and shifts brightness upward in state index.

Effect on top2 (TICT): the 90°-twisted geometry producing a very dark S1 is TICT-compatible, but the excitation energy of S1 does not show a clear stabilization upon twisting (e.g., S1 energy at 90° ≈4.3647 eV vs 0° ≈4.2281 eV), suggesting twist mainly quenches oscillator strength via decoupling rather than stabilizing a low-lying twisted CT minimum (a more TICT-specific hallmark).

Effect on an additional candidate (neutral aromatic): two independent torsions now show strong twist-induced darkening/state-ordering change, which further disfavors a torsion-robust LE/neutral-aromatic mechanism.

Key remaining discriminator for ICT vs TICT: whether any torsional coordinate yields energetic stabilization (lowering) of a twisted, dark CT-like state indicative of a TICT minimum (or at least a systematically lowering S1 energy with twist), versus twist acting mainly as an oscillator-quenching coordinate without stabilizing a lower-energy twisted emissive state.

Verifier supplementation: already sufficient for ICT-vs-neutral-aromatic (close-family solvatochromism/ICT precedent); not the limiting factor now.
Closure justification: still collecting for ICT vs TICT; the best next step is to fully parse the new torsion-snapshot per-angle state tables (states 1–3) to quantify state ordering/brightness trends across the second dihedral and compare energetics more cleanly.

Why the chosen next action is best now: parse-only extraction from the newly generated torsion snapshot bundle is the lowest-cost way to obtain complete per-angle state tables (energies, oscillator strengths, dominant transitions if present) for this second torsion coordinate, directly targeting the ICT-vs-TICT discriminator without invoking previously unstable CT-descriptor workflows.

Round 18/20 (rounds remaining incl. current: 3). Top1=ICT (0.58) vs Top2=TICT (0.22); additional candidates: neutral aromatic (0.10), unknown (0.08), ESIPT (0.02).

Latest evidence (microscopic parse_snapshot_outputs of round_17_torsion_snapshots; dihedral atoms [4,5,6,7]) strengthens ICT relative to TICT by providing complete per-angle state tables showing: (i) strong twist-induced oscillator quenching of S1 (f: 0° 0.3302 → 30° 0.2966 → 60° 0.1557 → 90° 0.0025), (ii) at 90° the first bright state shifts to S2 (pattern=lowest_state_is_dark_then_bright), and (iii) no clear energetic stabilization of the dark S1 upon twisting (S1 energies: 0° 4.2281 eV; 30° 4.3101 eV; 60° 4.4085 eV; 90° 4.3647 eV). This pattern is consistent with donor–acceptor decoupling that primarily removes oscillator strength and alters state ordering, rather than producing a distinctly stabilized twisted CT minimum.

Effect on top2 (TICT): still plausible because a dark lowest state appears at 90°, but weakened because the dark state is not lowered in energy vs near-planar geometries (no stabilization trend), and evidence remains vertical-only (no relaxed excited-state minimum).

Effect on an additional candidate (neutral aromatic): further weakened because robust LE-like behavior would not typically produce repeated lowest-state darkening and bright-state index shifts under enforced aryl–vinyl twisting.

Key remaining discriminator (ICT vs TICT): whether a twisted CT-like geometry is energetically favored on S1 (a stabilized twisted minimum) versus being mainly a dark, higher-energy vertical configuration reached only by forcing torsion. External precedent specifically about ICT vs TICT assignments in close analogs would be highly discriminative.

Verifier supplementation: sufficient for ICT vs neutral aromatic, but missing for ICT vs TICT. Closure justification: collecting (internal evidence is strong; external ICT-vs-TICT precedent still missing). Next action chooses verifier because it can directly address the remaining top1-vs-top2 mechanism split with external discriminator criteria for this scaffold.

Round-budget: current_round_index=19 of max_rounds=20 (rounds_remaining_including_current=2). Current top1=ICT (0.70) vs top2=TICT (0.15). Verifier supplementation for ICT__vs__TICT is now sufficient and close-family specific: (i) close-family D–A cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens are consistently assigned ICT (single CT band, positive solvatochromism, RIR/AIE) rather than a stabilized dark TICT minimum; (ii) classic TICT hallmarks require dual emission, viscosity-dependent CT band formation, and an energetically stabilized S1 minimum near ~90° twist; methoxy donors are cited as generally insufficient for stabilized TICT compared with dimethylamino donors; (iii) close-family mechanistic studies report S1 minima at moderate twist (not 90°) and explicitly note no TICT dual-band signature. This external evidence aligns with internal torsion-snapshot results: twisting strongly quenches oscillator strength and shifts the first bright state upward, but the dark S1 is not energetically stabilized with twist (argues against TICT dominance). Overall, verifier evidence is strong enough to materially advance closure and supports ICT over TICT; closure justification for ICT vs TICT is now sufficient for decisive finalization.

macro_reports.task_received: Assess macro-level structural evidence relevant to the current working hypothesis 'TICT'. Reuse the shared prepared structure context when available, summarize low-cost structural indicators only, and stay within current macro capability. Hypothesis uncertainty to keep in mind: Top1 (TICT) is uncertain because the molecule’s apparent high planarity and extended conjugation may favor a locally excited/neutral-aromatic emissive state with AIE arising from generic restriction of intramolecular motions rather than formation of a distinct twisted CT minimum; baseline S1 character/state ordering is needed to discriminate.

macro_reports.task_understanding: Provide macro-level (single-molecule, low-cost) structural evidence for/against the working hypothesis “TICT” using the shared prepared structure context (SMILES + geometry proxies), without performing excited-state calculations or aggregate/packing modeling.
macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the shared structure context, the molecule is a tri-aryl substituted alkene bearing a strong acceptor (–C#N) on the exocyclic vinyl carbon and a modest donor (anisole –OCH3) on one aryl ring: COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)Ph)Ph)cc1. This D–π–A motif plus multiple aryl–vinyl torsion candidates (torsion_candidate_count=4; rotatable_bond_count=4) provides plausible structural degrees of freedom for donor/acceptor decoupling via twisting, which is a prerequisite for TICT-like behavior. However, the geometry proxies indicate high overall planarity/extended conjugation in the selected conformer (planarity_proxy≈0.899; compactness_proxy≈0.902), suggesting the ground-state minimum may be relatively planar and conjugated, which can favor a locally excited (LE) emissive state rather than a distinctly twisted CT minimum. Conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0 with 10 conformers indicates some conformational variability exists, but within this macro capability we cannot confirm whether an orthogonal (near-90°) donor–acceptor twist is energetically accessible or whether a CT state is lowest in S1. Net macro-evidence: structural prerequisites for TICT (D/A substitution + torsional freedom) are present, but the current low-cost planarity indicator leans against an inherently pre-twisted architecture and is consistent with the Planner’s uncertainty about a more planar LE state dominating.
Capability limit note: Within the current macro scope, I can only use the provided prepared-structure metadata (planarity/compactness/torsion candidate counts, ring/rotatable bond counts, SMILES topology) to infer prerequisites and qualitative tendencies; I cannot compute excited-state CT character, barrier heights to twisting, or aggregation/packing effects that often decide TICT vs LE in AIE systems.

[focus_selection] Select the macro structural focus areas requested by the Planner instruction. [topology_analysis] Summarize rotor topology, ring systems, conjugation, and donor-acceptor layout. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize planarity, torsion, compactness, and conformer-dispersion proxies.

macro_reports.planner_readable_report:
Task completion: Task was completed only in a capability-limited contracted form. The agent returned bounded macro evidence, but it could not complete unsupported parts of the Planner instruction: Any determination of S1 state ordering (LE vs CT) or existence of a true TICT minimum without excited-state quantum chemistry; Quantitative torsion angle measurement/scan beyond the provided proxies (unless explicitly supplied by prepared context); Aggregate/packing-driven restriction analysis (RIM) or solid-state morphology predictions.
Task understanding: Interpret the Planner instruction as a macro-level structural analysis task for the current working hypothesis "TICT": Assess macro-level structural evidence relevant to the current working hypothesis 'TICT'. Reuse the shared prepared structure context when available, summarize low-cost structural indicators only, and stay within current macro capability. Hypothesis uncertainty to keep in mind: Top1 (TICT) is uncertain because the molecule’s apparent high planarity and extended conjugation may favor a locally excited/neutral-aromatic emissive state with AIE arising from generic restriction of intramolecular motions rather than formation of a distinct twisted CT minimum; baseline S1 character/state ordering is needed to discriminate.
Shared 3D structure context is available and reused from /datasets/workspace/mas_aie/var/runtime/tools/shared_structure/d39962f4411f/prepared_structure.xyz.
This agent should only summarize low-cost structural evidence and should not make a global mechanism judgment or recommend a system-level next action.
Reasoning summary: From the shared structure context, the molecule is a tri-aryl substituted alkene bearing a strong acceptor (–C#N) on the exocyclic vinyl carbon and a modest donor (anisole –OCH3) on one aryl ring: COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)Ph)Ph)cc1. This D–π–A motif plus multiple aryl–vinyl torsion candidates (torsion_candidate_count=4; rotatable_bond_count=4) provides plausible structural degrees of freedom for donor/acceptor decoupling via twisting, which is a prerequisite for TICT-like behavior. However, the geometry proxies indicate high overall planarity/extended conjugation in the selected conformer (planarity_proxy≈0.899; compactness_proxy≈0.902), suggesting the ground-state minimum may be relatively planar and conjugated, which can favor a locally excited (LE) emissive state rather than a distinctly twisted CT minimum. Conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0 with 10 conformers indicates some conformational variability exists, but within this macro capability we cannot confirm whether an orthogonal (near-90°) donor–acceptor twist is energetically accessible or whether a CT state is lowest in S1. Net macro-evidence: structural prerequisites for TICT (D/A substitution + torsional freedom) are present, but the current low-cost planarity indicator leans against an inherently pre-twisted architecture and is consistent with the Planner’s uncertainty about a more planar LE state dominating.
Capability limit note: Within the current macro scope, I can only use the provided prepared-structure metadata (planarity/compactness/torsion candidate counts, ring/rotatable bond counts, SMILES topology) to infer prerequisites and qualitative tendencies; I cannot compute excited-state CT character, barrier heights to twisting, or aggregation/packing effects that often decide TICT vs LE in AIE systems.
Execution plan: Use deterministic_macro_structure_scan to execute the bounded macro workflow with focus areas: Donor–acceptor partition proxy and functional group identification (–OCH3 donor, –C#N acceptor), Conjugation continuity across exocyclic C=C linking three aryl rings and nitrile substituent, Torsion candidates/rotatable bonds as enabling degrees of freedom for donor/acceptor decoupling, Planarity_proxy and conformer dispersion as low-cost indicators of ground-state geometry bias. Planned local steps: [shared_context_load] Load the shared prepared structure context and reuse its descriptors. [focus_selection] Select the macro structural focus areas requested by the Planner instruction. [topology_analysis] Summarize rotor topology, ring systems, conjugation, and donor-acceptor layout. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize planarity, torsion, compactness, and conformer-dispersion proxies.
Result summary: The macro scan recorded aromatic_atom_count=18, hetero_atom_count=2, branch_point_count=6, rotatable_bond_count=4, planarity_proxy=0.898693, compactness_proxy=0.901811, and conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.
Key proxies: aromatic_atom_count=18, hetero_atom_count=2, branch_point_count=6, conjugation_proxy=5.899, flexibility_proxy=8.0, rotatable_bond_count=4, aromatic_ring_count=3, ring_system_count=3, donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=0.5, planarity_proxy=0.898693, compactness_proxy=0.901811, conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.
Remaining local uncertainty: Macro evidence alone cannot resolve excited-state relaxation behavior or external consistency; unresolved local gap: Macro evidence is limited to single-molecule low-cost structural and geometry proxies, so it still cannot resolve excited-state relaxation behavior, aggregation-state packing, or external consistency.

reasoning_output.task_understanding: Run one registry-backed targeted localized-orbital analysis using the existing baseline artifact bundle "round_01_baseline_bundle" as the fixed electronic-structure source. Do not re-optimize geometries. Do not perform excited-state relaxation or torsion scans. The analysis should prioritize frontier-orbital localization relevant to the low-lying bright states (capture the principal state-window around S1/S2 and inspect HOMO–LUMO-adjacent localized orbitals) and extract any donor-vs-acceptor partition metrics that the localized-orbital workflow can provide.
reasoning_output.reasoning_summary: The requested action exactly matches the registry action run_targeted_localized_orbital_analysis. The registry accepts artifact_bundle_id, optimize_ground_state, state_window, and related params; it is permitted to reuse a baseline_bundle and to request fixed-geometry (optimize_ground_state=false) localized-orbital analysis. Orbital-index-level targeting (e.g., occupied 81/82, virtual 83) is a requested analysis focus but is not an explicit action parameter in the registry; the worker will therefore request the nearby vertical-state window and internally prioritize frontier orbitals during post-processing within the action's supported deliverables.

microscopic_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: The requested action maps directly to the registry action run_targeted_density_population_analysis whose allowed parameters include artifact_bundle_id and state_window. The registry enforces fixed-geometry bounded follow-ups (optimize_ground_state default=false is supported) and does not perform excited-state relaxation or torsion scans, matching the Planner constraints. I will request reuse of the specified artifact bundle and a targeted state_window [1,2]. Including a discovery step (list_artifact_bundles) is optional but helpful to validate the specified bundle ID before execution.
Capability limit note: This bounded route cannot perform excited-state relaxation or relaxed torsion scans (those are unsupported). The action will report whichever density/population observables the backend produces (per-atom Mulliken-like charges or bounded raw density/population observables). If explicit fragment-partition CT metrics (qCT, hole–electron separation, NTO-based metrics) are unavailable in the produced outputs, the result will include per-atom and any available fragment charge summaries as the closest supported proxies; any missing CT-specific descriptors will be reported as unavailable.
Failure policy: If the specified artifact_bundle_id is not found or lacks the necessary snapshot files, report that explicitly and fall back to listing available artifact bundles (via list_artifact_bundles) and request Planner guidance. If requested CT-specific descriptors are absent from computed outputs, the run will still return available per-atom and raw density/population observables and explicitly mark the missing CT descriptors so Planner can decide on alternative follow-ups.

microscopic_reports.reasoning_summary:
Local reasoning summary: The registry exposes inspect_raw_artifact_bundle which exactly matches a parse-only raw-artifact inspection. To honor "Do not run new calculations" we must set perform_new_calculation=false and reuse_existing_artifacts_only=true (defaults align). We will request explicit observable families likely to contain CT/state-character information via requested_observable_scope so the inspector reports availability/unavailability of each item rather than inferring values.
Capability limit note: inspect_raw_artifact_bundle reports raw-file coverage and which observable families are extractable from existing files, but it does not itself compute or infer chemistry-specific CT descriptors. If requested observables are absent in the bundle, the action can only report that absence — it cannot reconstruct missing CT metrics by computation.
Failure policy: If the bundle is not found or access fails, return a short structured error indicating "artifact_bundle_not_found" or "bundle_access_error". If requested observable families are present but in unexpected formats, report them as "present_but_unparsable" with filename examples. Do not launch any new calculations under any failure branch.

Provide 2–5 most relevant sources or well-established review statements, and summarize what evidence would favor ICT over neutral-aromatic for this scaffold.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4228–4238 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14918 (cyanovinyl-aryl AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: ICT character in cyano-vinyl-aryl AIEgens with donor–acceptor substitution
evidence_cards.observation: Tang and coworkers systematically reviewed cyanovinyl-arene AIEgens bearing electron-donor (alkoxy, amino) and electron-acceptor (cyano) groups. These compounds are described as having intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) character, evidenced by pronounced positive solvatochromism (emission red-shifting and broadening with increasing solvent polarity), large Stokes shifts (>100 nm in polar solvents), and significant ground-to-excited-state dipole moment changes. The cyano group acts as the acceptor and the aryloxy/amino group as the donor in a push-pull alkene framework. Restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) in aggregates locks the conformation and activates the ICT emission that is quenched in solution by non-radiative decay via rotation.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The scaffold described (cyano-vinyl-aryl with alkoxy donor) is structurally analogous to the query molecule (methoxy-phenyl donor, cyano acceptor, triaryl-alkenyl core). The ICT assignment in this family is based on solvatochromism and large Stokes shifts, which are the key discriminators between ICT and neutral-aromatic (LE) emission for this closure pair.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT emission with RIR-based AIE; cyano group is acceptor, alkoxy-aryl is donor; positive solvatochromism reported
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution photophysics in solvents of varying polarity (hexane to DMSO); aggregate emission in THF/water mixtures

evidence_cards.source: J. Mater. Chem. C 2014, 2, 3885–3894; Dyes Pigm. 2018, 149, 491–500 (methoxy-cyano-stilbene and triarylethylene AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: Methoxy-cyano-stilbene analogs: ICT vs LE assignment based on solvatochromism and aggregate blue-shift
evidence_cards.observation: Studies on methoxy-substituted cyano-stilbenes and related triarylethylenes report that compounds with a para-methoxy donor and a cyano acceptor on the vinyl bridge show moderate-to-strong positive solvatochromism (emission shift of 30–80 nm from nonpolar to polar solvents), consistent with ICT character. In aggregates or solid state, emission is typically blue-shifted relative to polar solution emission, attributed to restricted conformational relaxation suppressing full CT state stabilization. Compounds lacking the donor–acceptor asymmetry (symmetric triarylethylenes without push-pull substitution) show much weaker solvatochromism and are assigned LE/neutral-aromatic emission. The presence of both methoxy (donor) and cyano (acceptor) on the same conjugated alkene is described as a sufficient structural criterion for ICT character.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The query molecule has a para-methoxy-phenyl donor and a cyano group on the vinyl carbon, directly matching the structural criterion described for ICT assignment in this family. The aggregate blue-shift relative to polar solution is a reported hallmark that can be checked experimentally.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT assigned for methoxy-donor + cyano-acceptor vinyl systems; LE/neutral-aromatic for symmetric analogs without push-pull substitution
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measured in cyclohexane, toluene, THF, DCM, acetonitrile, DMSO; aggregate emission in THF/water

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492–14497 (tetraphenylethylene and cyano-substituted analogs, Tang group); Chem. Commun. 2016, 52, 9462
evidence_cards.title: Neutral-aromatic (LE) emission in symmetric triarylethylenes without push-pull substitution
evidence_cards.observation: Symmetric triarylethylenes such as tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and its close analogs without donor–acceptor substitution are reported to emit from locally excited (LE) or neutral-aromatic states, with negligible solvatochromism (emission shift <10 nm across solvents of widely varying polarity). These compounds show no significant dipole moment change upon excitation and their emission is assigned to restricted intramolecular rotation (RIR) of the phenyl rotors without CT character. Introduction of a strong donor (amino, alkoxy) and acceptor (cyano, nitro) breaks the symmetry and introduces ICT character, shifting the mechanistic assignment.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card establishes the structural boundary between neutral-aromatic (LE/RIR) and ICT assignments for the triarylethylene family. The query molecule is asymmetric (methoxy donor + cyano acceptor), placing it outside the symmetric LE-emitter category described here.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: LE/neutral-aromatic emission for symmetric triarylethylenes; ICT introduced by donor-acceptor asymmetry
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism experiments in multiple solvents; comparison of symmetric vs asymmetric analogs

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2020, 11, 3709–3716; Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2019, 21, 9225 (NTO/hole-electron analysis of D-A AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: Pairwise discriminator: quantitative CT descriptors (NTO, hole-electron separation, qCT) for D-A AIEgens
evidence_cards.observation: For donor–acceptor (D-A) conjugated emitters, natural transition orbital (NTO) analysis and hole–electron separation metrics (qCT, D_CT) are reported as decisive discriminators between ICT and LE states. ICT states show spatially separated hole (on donor) and electron (on acceptor) NTOs with D_CT > 1.5 Å and qCT > 0.3 e. LE states show overlapping hole and electron NTOs localized on the same aromatic unit with D_CT < 0.5 Å. For cyano-vinyl-aryl systems with methoxy donors, TD-DFT/NTO calculations consistently assign the S1 state as ICT with D_CT values of 1.5–3.0 Å. Solvatochromism (positive, >30 nm shift from nonpolar to polar solvent) is described as the experimental proxy for ICT, while <10 nm shift indicates LE/neutral-aromatic. The absence of computational data (as in the current case where Amesp failed) means solvatochromism experiments are the most accessible discriminator.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the main gap: it describes the quantitative CT descriptors (NTO, D_CT, qCT) that would decisively assign ICT vs LE, and provides the experimental proxy (solvatochromism magnitude) that can be used when computational data are unavailable. It also gives threshold values for interpretation.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT: D_CT > 1.5 Å, qCT > 0.3 e, solvatochromism > 30 nm; LE: D_CT < 0.5 Å, solvatochromism < 10 nm
evidence_cards.experimental_context: TD-DFT NTO analysis and solvatochromism experiments on D-A conjugated emitters including cyano-vinyl-aryl scaffolds

It also gives threshold values for interpretation.

evidence_cards.source: Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4228–4238 (Tang et al., AIE review); J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2012, 134, 14918 (cyanovinyl-aryl AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: ICT character in cyano-vinyl-aryl AIEgens with donor–acceptor substitution
evidence_cards.observation: Tang and coworkers systematically reviewed cyanovinyl-arene AIEgens bearing electron-donor (alkoxy, amino) and electron-acceptor (cyano) groups. These compounds are described as having intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) character, evidenced by pronounced positive solvatochromism (emission red-shifting and broadening with increasing solvent polarity), large Stokes shifts (>100 nm in polar solvents), and significant ground-to-excited-state dipole moment changes. The cyano group acts as the acceptor and the aryloxy/amino group as the donor in a push-pull alkene framework. Restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) in aggregates locks the conformation and activates the ICT emission that is quenched in solution by non-radiative decay via rotation.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The scaffold described (cyano-vinyl-aryl with alkoxy donor) is structurally analogous to the query molecule (methoxy-phenyl donor, cyano acceptor, triaryl-alkenyl core). The ICT assignment in this family is based on solvatochromism and large Stokes shifts, which are the key discriminators between ICT and neutral-aromatic (LE) emission for this closure pair.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT emission with RIR-based AIE; cyano group is acceptor, alkoxy-aryl is donor; positive solvatochromism reported
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution photophysics in solvents of varying polarity (hexane to DMSO); aggregate emission in THF/water mixtures

evidence_cards.source: J. Mater. Chem. C 2014, 2, 3885–3894; Dyes Pigm. 2018, 149, 491–500 (methoxy-cyano-stilbene and triarylethylene AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: Methoxy-cyano-stilbene analogs: ICT vs LE assignment based on solvatochromism and aggregate blue-shift
evidence_cards.observation: Studies on methoxy-substituted cyano-stilbenes and related triarylethylenes report that compounds with a para-methoxy donor and a cyano acceptor on the vinyl bridge show moderate-to-strong positive solvatochromism (emission shift of 30–80 nm from nonpolar to polar solvents), consistent with ICT character. In aggregates or solid state, emission is typically blue-shifted relative to polar solution emission, attributed to restricted conformational relaxation suppressing full CT state stabilization. Compounds lacking the donor–acceptor asymmetry (symmetric triarylethylenes without push-pull substitution) show much weaker solvatochromism and are assigned LE/neutral-aromatic emission. The presence of both methoxy (donor) and cyano (acceptor) on the same conjugated alkene is described as a sufficient structural criterion for ICT character.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The query molecule has a para-methoxy-phenyl donor and a cyano group on the vinyl carbon, directly matching the structural criterion described for ICT assignment in this family. The aggregate blue-shift relative to polar solution is a reported hallmark that can be checked experimentally.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT assigned for methoxy-donor + cyano-acceptor vinyl systems; LE/neutral-aromatic for symmetric analogs without push-pull substitution
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism measured in cyclohexane, toluene, THF, DCM, acetonitrile, DMSO; aggregate emission in THF/water

evidence_cards.source: Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 2015, 54, 14492–14497 (tetraphenylethylene and cyano-substituted analogs, Tang group); Chem. Commun. 2016, 52, 9462
evidence_cards.title: Neutral-aromatic (LE) emission in symmetric triarylethylenes without push-pull substitution
evidence_cards.observation: Symmetric triarylethylenes such as tetraphenylethylene (TPE) and its close analogs without donor–acceptor substitution are reported to emit from locally excited (LE) or neutral-aromatic states, with negligible solvatochromism (emission shift <10 nm across solvents of widely varying polarity). These compounds show no significant dipole moment change upon excitation and their emission is assigned to restricted intramolecular rotation (RIR) of the phenyl rotors without CT character. Introduction of a strong donor (amino, alkoxy) and acceptor (cyano, nitro) breaks the symmetry and introduces ICT character, shifting the mechanistic assignment.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card establishes the structural boundary between neutral-aromatic (LE/RIR) and ICT assignments for the triarylethylene family. The query molecule is asymmetric (methoxy donor + cyano acceptor), placing it outside the symmetric LE-emitter category described here.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: LE/neutral-aromatic emission for symmetric triarylethylenes; ICT introduced by donor-acceptor asymmetry
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solvatochromism experiments in multiple solvents; comparison of symmetric vs asymmetric analogs

evidence_cards.source: J. Phys. Chem. Lett. 2020, 11, 3709–3716; Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys. 2019, 21, 9225 (NTO/hole-electron analysis of D-A AIEgens)
evidence_cards.title: Pairwise discriminator: quantitative CT descriptors (NTO, hole-electron separation, qCT) for D-A AIEgens
evidence_cards.observation: For donor–acceptor (D-A) conjugated emitters, natural transition orbital (NTO) analysis and hole–electron separation metrics (qCT, D_CT) are reported as decisive discriminators between ICT and LE states. ICT states show spatially separated hole (on donor) and electron (on acceptor) NTOs with D_CT > 1.5 Å and qCT > 0.3 e. LE states show overlapping hole and electron NTOs localized on the same aromatic unit with D_CT < 0.5 Å. For cyano-vinyl-aryl systems with methoxy donors, TD-DFT/NTO calculations consistently assign the S1 state as ICT with D_CT values of 1.5–3.0 Å. Solvatochromism (positive, >30 nm shift from nonpolar to polar solvent) is described as the experimental proxy for ICT, while <10 nm shift indicates LE/neutral-aromatic. The absence of computational data (as in the current case where Amesp failed) means solvatochromism experiments are the most accessible discriminator.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card directly addresses the main gap: it describes the quantitative CT descriptors (NTO, D_CT, qCT) that would decisively assign ICT vs LE, and provides the experimental proxy (solvatochromism magnitude) that can be used when computational data are unavailable. It also gives threshold values for interpretation.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: ICT: D_CT > 1.5 Å, qCT > 0.3 e, solvatochromism > 30 nm; LE: D_CT < 0.5 Å, solvatochromism < 10 nm
evidence_cards.experimental_context: TD-DFT NTO analysis and solvatochromism experiments on D-A conjugated emitters including cyano-vinyl-aryl scaffolds

evidence_cards.source: Tang et al., J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2001, 123, 9482; Luo et al., Chem. Commun. 2001; and subsequent AIE review literature (Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4228)
evidence_cards.title: ICT in cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens: planar ICT state assignment in D-A substituted tetraphenylethylene and cyano-stilbene analogs
evidence_cards.observation: Review and primary literature on cyanovinyl-substituted triarylethylenes (including 1,1-dicyano-2,2-bis(aryl)ethylenes and related push-pull stilbenes) consistently assign emission to an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) state that retains significant oscillator strength. These systems show strong positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents), large Stokes shifts, and emission quenching in solution attributed to rotational deactivation rather than to a stabilized dark TICT minimum. In the solid state or viscous media, restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) restores emission from the ICT/LE-mixed state. No dual emission or viscosity-independent CT emission (hallmarks of TICT) is reported for this scaffold class.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the champion hypothesis (ICT) for the closest structural family. The mechanistic assignment in these analogs is ICT with RIR-based AIE, not TICT, providing precedent for the current scaffold.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Emission in cyanovinyl triarylethylene D-A systems arises from an ICT state with retained oscillator strength; solution quenching is due to rotational deactivation (RIR mechanism), not stabilization of a dark TICT minimum.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution photoluminescence in solvents of varying polarity; solid-state and aggregate emission; viscosity-dependent studies in glycerol/ethanol mixtures.

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, B. J. J. Phys. Chem. A 2000, 104, 5551; Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971; Zachariasse et al., J. Phys. Chem. A 2004, 108, 1275
evidence_cards.title: Hallmarks distinguishing TICT from ICT: dual emission, viscosity dependence, and computational S1 minimum at ~90° dihedral
evidence_cards.observation: The TICT mechanism, as established for DMABN and related aminobenzonitrile systems, requires: (1) dual fluorescence bands (LE + CT) in polar solvents, (2) a viscosity-dependent CT band intensity (TICT formation rate limited by rotational diffusion), (3) computational evidence of an S1 potential energy minimum at ~90° twist angle that is energetically stabilized relative to the Franck-Condon geometry, and (4) a donor group with a lone pair capable of full decoupling at 90° (e.g., dimethylamino). Zachariasse et al. further showed that systems lacking a true S1 minimum at 90° (i.e., where the twisted geometry is a transition state or saddle point rather than a minimum) do not exhibit TICT emission even if they show CT character. Methoxy donors, unlike dimethylamino groups, have much weaker lone-pair donation and do not readily form stabilized TICT states.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the key discriminator criteria between ICT and TICT. The current molecule has a methoxy donor (weak lone-pair donor, unlike dimethylamino) and no reported dual emission, making TICT assignment mechanistically less plausible by these established criteria.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires a stabilized S1 minimum at ~90° twist, dual emission in polar solvents, and viscosity-dependent CT band; methoxy donors are generally insufficient to generate stabilized TICT states unlike dimethylamino donors.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Established from DMABN and aminobenzonitrile model systems; temperature- and viscosity-dependent fluorescence; computational PES scans along twist coordinate.

evidence_cards.source: Hu, R. et al., ACS Nano 2013, 7, 10258; Zhao, Z. et al., Chem. Sci. 2014, 5, 3494; and related push-pull AIEgen mechanistic studies
evidence_cards.title: ICT-AIE co-mechanism in D-A triarylethylenes: polarity-dependent emission without TICT dual-band signature
evidence_cards.observation: Studies on D-A substituted tetraphenylethylene and related triarylethylene AIEgens bearing electron-donating groups (alkoxy, amino) and electron-withdrawing groups (cyano, carbonyl) report a combined ICT-AIE mechanism. In solution, emission is quenched by intramolecular rotation; in aggregates or viscous media, a single broad CT emission band is restored. Emission maxima shift bathochromically with increasing solvent polarity (positive solvatochromism, Stokes shift increases), consistent with ICT. No secondary long-wavelength band attributable to a TICT state is observed. Computational studies on these systems locate the S1 minimum at a geometry close to the Franck-Condon structure (moderate twist, not 90°), ruling out a stabilized TICT minimum as the emissive state.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the pairwise discriminator question for the closest structural family to the current molecule. The absence of dual emission and the location of the S1 minimum away from 90° in D-A triarylethylene analogs are key observations distinguishing ICT from TICT for this scaffold class.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: D-A triarylethylene AIEgens with alkoxy donors and cyano acceptors emit from a single ICT state (not a TICT minimum); S1 minimum is at moderate twist geometry; no dual emission observed in polar solvents.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity (hexane to DMSO); aggregate/solid-state emission; TD-DFT geometry optimization of S1 state.

evidence_cards.source: Tang et al., J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2001, 123, 9482; Luo et al., Chem. Commun. 2001; and subsequent AIE review literature (Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44, 4228)
evidence_cards.title: ICT in cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens: planar ICT state assignment in D-A substituted tetraphenylethylene and cyano-stilbene analogs
evidence_cards.observation: Review and primary literature on cyanovinyl-substituted triarylethylenes (including 1,1-dicyano-2,2-bis(aryl)ethylenes and related push-pull stilbenes) consistently assign emission to an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) state that retains significant oscillator strength. These systems show strong positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents), large Stokes shifts, and emission quenching in solution attributed to rotational deactivation rather than to a stabilized dark TICT minimum. In the solid state or viscous media, restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) restores emission from the ICT/LE-mixed state. No dual emission or viscosity-independent CT emission (hallmarks of TICT) is reported for this scaffold class.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the champion hypothesis (ICT) for the closest structural family. The mechanistic assignment in these analogs is ICT with RIR-based AIE, not TICT, providing precedent for the current scaffold.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Emission in cyanovinyl triarylethylene D-A systems arises from an ICT state with retained oscillator strength; solution quenching is due to rotational deactivation (RIR mechanism), not stabilization of a dark TICT minimum.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution photoluminescence in solvents of varying polarity; solid-state and aggregate emission; viscosity-dependent studies in glycerol/ethanol mixtures.

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, B. J. J. Phys. Chem. A 2000, 104, 5551; Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971; Zachariasse et al., J. Phys. Chem. A 2004, 108, 1275
evidence_cards.title: Hallmarks distinguishing TICT from ICT: dual emission, viscosity dependence, and computational S1 minimum at ~90° dihedral
evidence_cards.observation: The TICT mechanism, as established for DMABN and related aminobenzonitrile systems, requires: (1) dual fluorescence bands (LE + CT) in polar solvents, (2) a viscosity-dependent CT band intensity (TICT formation rate limited by rotational diffusion), (3) computational evidence of an S1 potential energy minimum at ~90° twist angle that is energetically stabilized relative to the Franck-Condon geometry, and (4) a donor group with a lone pair capable of full decoupling at 90° (e.g., dimethylamino). Zachariasse et al. further showed that systems lacking a true S1 minimum at 90° (i.e., where the twisted geometry is a transition state or saddle point rather than a minimum) do not exhibit TICT emission even if they show CT character. Methoxy donors, unlike dimethylamino groups, have much weaker lone-pair donation and do not readily form stabilized TICT states.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides the key discriminator criteria between ICT and TICT. The current molecule has a methoxy donor (weak lone-pair donor, unlike dimethylamino) and no reported dual emission, making TICT assignment mechanistically less plausible by these established criteria.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires a stabilized S1 minimum at ~90° twist, dual emission in polar solvents, and viscosity-dependent CT band; methoxy donors are generally insufficient to generate stabilized TICT states unlike dimethylamino donors.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Established from DMABN and aminobenzonitrile model systems; temperature- and viscosity-dependent fluorescence; computational PES scans along twist coordinate.

evidence_cards.source: Hu, R. et al., ACS Nano 2013, 7, 10258; Zhao, Z. et al., Chem. Sci. 2014, 5, 3494; and related push-pull AIEgen mechanistic studies
evidence_cards.title: ICT-AIE co-mechanism in D-A triarylethylenes: polarity-dependent emission without TICT dual-band signature
evidence_cards.observation: Studies on D-A substituted tetraphenylethylene and related triarylethylene AIEgens bearing electron-donating groups (alkoxy, amino) and electron-withdrawing groups (cyano, carbonyl) report a combined ICT-AIE mechanism. In solution, emission is quenched by intramolecular rotation; in aggregates or viscous media, a single broad CT emission band is restored. Emission maxima shift bathochromically with increasing solvent polarity (positive solvatochromism, Stokes shift increases), consistent with ICT. No secondary long-wavelength band attributable to a TICT state is observed. Computational studies on these systems locate the S1 minimum at a geometry close to the Franck-Condon structure (moderate twist, not 90°), ruling out a stabilized TICT minimum as the emissive state.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Directly addresses the pairwise discriminator question for the closest structural family to the current molecule. The absence of dual emission and the location of the S1 minimum away from 90° in D-A triarylethylene analogs are key observations distinguishing ICT from TICT for this scaffold class.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: D-A triarylethylene AIEgens with alkoxy donors and cyano acceptors emit from a single ICT state (not a TICT minimum); S1 minimum is at moderate twist geometry; no dual emission observed in polar solvents.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Solution fluorescence in solvents of varying polarity (hexane to DMSO); aggregate/solid-state emission; TD-DFT geometry optimization of S1 state.

agent_reports.task_understanding: Provide macro-level (single-molecule, low-cost) structural evidence for/against the working hypothesis “TICT” using the shared prepared structure context (SMILES + geometry proxies), without performing excited-state calculations or aggregate/packing modeling.
agent_reports.reasoning_summary:
From the shared structure context, the molecule is a tri-aryl substituted alkene bearing a strong acceptor (–C#N) on the exocyclic vinyl carbon and a modest donor (anisole –OCH3) on one aryl ring: COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)Ph)Ph)cc1. This D–π–A motif plus multiple aryl–vinyl torsion candidates (torsion_candidate_count=4; rotatable_bond_count=4) provides plausible structural degrees of freedom for donor/acceptor decoupling via twisting, which is a prerequisite for TICT-like behavior. However, the geometry proxies indicate high overall planarity/extended conjugation in the selected conformer (planarity_proxy≈0.899; compactness_proxy≈0.902), suggesting the ground-state minimum may be relatively planar and conjugated, which can favor a locally excited (LE) emissive state rather than a distinctly twisted CT minimum. Conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0 with 10 conformers indicates some conformational variability exists, but within this macro capability we cannot confirm whether an orthogonal (near-90°) donor–acceptor twist is energetically accessible or whether a CT state is lowest in S1. Net macro-evidence: structural prerequisites for TICT (D/A substitution + torsional freedom) are present, but the current low-cost planarity indicator leans against an inherently pre-twisted architecture and is consistent with the Planner’s uncertainty about a more planar LE state dominating.
Capability limit note: Within the current macro scope, I can only use the provided prepared-structure metadata (planarity/compactness/torsion candidate counts, ring/rotatable bond counts, SMILES topology) to infer prerequisites and qualitative tendencies; I cannot compute excited-state CT character, barrier heights to twisting, or aggregation/packing effects that often decide TICT vs LE in AIE systems.

Planned local steps: [shared_context_load] Load the shared prepared structure context and reuse its descriptors. [focus_selection] Select the macro structural focus areas requested by the Planner instruction. [topology_analysis] Summarize rotor topology, ring systems, conjugation, and donor-acceptor layout. [geometry_proxy_analysis] Summarize planarity, torsion, compactness, and conformer-dispersion proxies.

hypothesis_pool.rationale: High planarity proxy (~0.899) and a bright lowest vertical excitation (S1 f~0.288) remain consistent with a predominantly locally excited (LE) aromatic/triaryl-alkenyl chromophore where AIE can be dominated by restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) rather than strong CT.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Clear push–pull D–π–A motif (anisole donor, cyano acceptor) and a sizable ground-state dipole (~8.17 D) support possible CT involvement, but the attempted CT-descriptor extraction did not yield the key quantitative hole–electron metrics needed to confirm ICT character for S1/S2.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Rotatable bonds/torsion candidates (4) provide geometric freedom for twisting, but current evidence still lacks any direct indicator of a twisted CT state (or dark/weakly allowed lowest state pattern) dominating emissive behavior.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Increases slightly because the targeted parse-only CT extraction failed to produce the decisive CT observables, leaving open the possibility of state-mixing or non-obvious excited-state character not captured by current outputs (energies/f-values/dominant transitions/dipole).

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No plausible intramolecular proton donor/acceptor arrangement (methoxy O and nitrile N only), making ESIPT very unlikely.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: High planarity_proxy (~0.899) plus a bright lowest vertical state (S1 f~0.288) and the fact that S1/S2 share the same primary excitation family (dominant transition signature 82→83) remain most consistent with a predominantly delocalized π–π* / LE-type excitation on a triaryl-alkenyl scaffold (AIE plausibly via generic RIM rather than strong CT).

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Push–pull D–π–A motif (anisole donor, cyano acceptor) and sizable S0 dipole (~8.17 D) keep ICT plausible, but repeated failure to obtain quantitative CT descriptors (qCT, hole–electron separation/overlap, NTO/attachment–detachment) prevents confirmation; current dominant-transition-only evidence does not uniquely indicate charge separation.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Rotatable bonds/torsion candidates (4) provide geometric freedom, but current evidence lacks a hallmark dark/weakly allowed lowest-state pattern or any twisting/CT-specific diagnostics; planar ground-state proxy and bright S1 reduce specificity for a TICT-dominated emissive pathway.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Upweighted because two attempts to obtain CT metrics (parse-only CT extraction; targeted state characterization) produced only proxy-level outputs, leaving substantial residual uncertainty about excited-state character (possible state mixing or CT not captured by available descriptors).

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No credible intramolecular proton donor/acceptor pair in the structure (methoxy + nitrile only), making ESIPT very unlikely.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Verifier supplementation (close-family literature on methoxy-donor + cyano-acceptor cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens) repeatedly assigns ICT based on positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts, and donor–acceptor asymmetry; this directly matches the queried push–pull topology. Internal vertical-state pattern (bright lowest state) is compatible with an allowed ICT/LE-mixed transition and does not contradict ICT.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal structural/vertical proxies (high planarity_proxy ~0.899; lowest state bright) remain consistent with LE/neutral-aromatic competitiveness, but verifier evidence indicates that closely related asymmetric methoxy–cyano triaryl-alkenyl systems are typically ICT/solvatochromic, while LE/neutral-aromatic behavior is more characteristic of symmetric triarylethylenes without push–pull substitution.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Multiple internal attempts to obtain decisive CT descriptors (qCT, hole–electron separation, NTO/attachment–detachment) were unavailable or failed (LMO backend error), so there remains residual uncertainty about the actual excited-state character for this specific molecule without a molecule-specific CT observable.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Rotatable bonds/torsion candidates exist, but neither internal vertical-state ordering (lowest state is bright) nor verifier evidence specifically indicates a twisted CT minimum dominating emission; TICT remains possible but not the best-supported CT-type mechanism from current evidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No credible proton-transfer donor/acceptor pair in the structure (methoxy + nitrile only); no verifier evidence suggests ESIPT for this scaffold.

Focus specifically on: (1) whether analogous methoxy-cyano-stilbene / triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens are described as ICT/solvatochromic vs LE-dominated; (2) reported hallmarks: solvatochromism strength, large dipole change, large Stokes shift, emission broadening/red-shift with solvent polarity, aggregate-state blue/red shifts; (3) any explicit mechanistic assignment (ICT vs LE vs TICT) for close analogs (same cyano-vinyl-phenyl core with anisole donor). Provide 2–5 most relevant sources or well-established review statements, and summarize what evidence would favor ICT over neutral-aromatic for this scaffold.
