hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Close-family verifier evidence for methoxy-donor + cyano-acceptor cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens strongly supports ICT (positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts, large dipole change). Latest microscopic follow-up still shows a large S0 dipole (~8.17 D) and bright S1, consistent with an allowed CT-mixed transition, but did not deliver a molecule-specific excited-state CT proxy (no fragment charge shift / qCT / hole–electron metrics).

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal proxies still allow an LE/neutral-aromatic assignment: high planarity_proxy (~0.899) and a bright lowest vertical excitation with shared excitation-family signature (82→83) can indicate a delocalized π–π* manifold. Lack of molecule-specific CT metrics keeps this challenger viable despite the push–pull scaffold.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Multiple internal attempts to obtain decisive CT observables (NTO/hole–electron/qCT; LMO; fragment charge redistribution) have returned proxy-only outputs or failed; the latest density/population analysis produced density-matrix/gross-population/Mayer outputs but not an interpretable donor-vs-acceptor CT discriminator, sustaining residual uncertainty for this exact molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Torsional freedom exists (4 rotatable bonds), but the observed vertical pattern (lowest state is bright) and absence of twisting/CT-minimum evidence keep TICT secondary relative to planar ICT/LE interpretations.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No plausible intramolecular proton-transfer motif (no phenolic OH / intramolecular H-bond donor–acceptor pair).

working_memory.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT is currently the best-supported hypothesis primarily due to close-family external solvatochromism-based ICT assignments for this push–pull methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl motif. However, internal molecule-specific CT diagnostics remain unavailable (qCT/hole–electron/NTO/fragment charge shift), so neutral-aromatic LE-like emission remains a credible runner-up given planarity and bright, shared-family low-lying excitations.
working_memory.capability_assessment: Verifier is already high-information and sufficient for ICT vs neutral-aromatic at the close-family level. Microscopic calculations reliably provide vertical energies/f-values and dominant transitions, but several direct CT descriptor routes have been unavailable/unstable (NTO/hole–electron/qCT missing; LMO backend failure). The density/population route returned only generic density-matrix/population observables without an immediately interpretable donor–acceptor CT measure. Next step should therefore switch to a different supported observable family (transition-dipole/transition-density descriptors) rather than retrying stalled routes.
working_memory.stagnation_assessment: No global stagnation, but internal closure has been partially stalled because multiple CT-specific descriptor pathways have not produced a decisive molecule-specific discriminator. This round adds some supportive consistency checks (dipole, state signatures) but does not materially reduce the ICT-vs-LE ambiguity; switching the microscopic observable family is warranted.
working_memory.contraction_reason: Avoid reattempting known-problematic LMO/NTO/qCT extraction routes; use a single alternative, registry-supported microscopic observable (transition dipole / transition-density descriptors) that can serve as a CT proxy for the ICT vs neutral-aromatic decision.
working_memory.information_gain_assessment: Medium expected information gain: transition-dipole/transition-density descriptors may provide a workable CT proxy within supported registry actions after multiple direct CT-descriptor pathways were unavailable.
working_memory.gap_trend: Slowly narrowing: verifier strongly constrains the prior toward ICT, but internal closure remains limited by missing molecule-specific CT observables; next step changes the microscopic observable family to target that gap.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Slightly downweighted vs prior because the latest targeted density/population analysis did not produce the intended donor–acceptor charge-redistribution proxy; still favored by sufficient close-family solvatochromism-based ICT precedent.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Slightly upweighted because internal evidence remains consistent with delocalized LE-like excitations and the ICT discriminator remains unobserved for this exact molecule.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Upweighted slightly because repeated internal attempts to obtain decisive CT metrics continue to return proxy-only outputs, sustaining residual uncertainty.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Slightly downweighted/held because no new evidence indicates a twisted CT minimum or a dark low-lying CT state pattern.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Unchanged minimal due to lack of structural proton-transfer capability.

working_memory.closure_justification_summary: External precedent strongly favors ICT for this push–pull scaffold, but internal closure still lacks a molecule-specific CT proxy. Latest density/population follow-up produced only generic density/population observables without donor–acceptor charge-shift interpretation. Next: attempt a different internal CT proxy via transition-dipole/transition-density descriptors for S1/S2.

working_memory.information_gain_assessment: Medium expected information gain: RIS state characterization is one of the remaining distinct in-registry routes that may produce state-character/CT descriptors after prior CT/charge/population/dipole attempts were proxy-only.
working_memory.gap_trend: Slowly narrowing due to strong verifier prior; internally still largely unresolved because molecule-specific CT metrics remain unavailable. Next step targets the same gap with a different internal route.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Slightly downweighted because the latest internal charge-analysis run still did not yield an interpretable excited-state CT/charge-redistribution metric; remains top due to strong close-family solvatochromism-based precedent.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Held/slightly supported because internal proxies (planarity + bright low-lying transitions with shared excitation family) remain consistent with LE-like emission and no molecule-specific CT metric contradicts it.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Upweighted because repeated internal attempts to extract decisive CT observables continue to return proxy-only or non-interpretable outputs, leaving open state-mixing/descriptor-missing scenarios.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted/kept low because there is still no specific evidence for a twisted CT minimum dominating emission (no lowest-dark hallmark, no torsion/CT-minimum evidence).
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Kept minimal because the structure lacks an ESIPT-capable donor/acceptor pair.

working_memory.closure_justification_summary: External precedent strongly favors ICT for this push–pull scaffold, but internal closure still lacks a molecule-specific CT metric. hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Still best-supported by strong close-family literature: asymmetric methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens are typically assigned ICT based on positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts, and donor–acceptor asymmetry. Latest RIS state characterization did not provide direct CT metrics (NTO/hole–electron/qCT), so ICT remains a prior-driven (not molecule-specifically proven) best hypothesis.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal computed signatures remain compatible with an LE/neutral-aromatic manifold: high planarity proxy (~0.899), bright lowest state, and shared dominant transition signature (82→83) across S1/S2. Absence of molecule-specific CT separation metrics prevents ruling this out.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Increased because multiple distinct microscopic routes intended to yield CT discriminators (CT-descriptor extraction, targeted state characterization, density/population, transition dipole, charge analysis, RIS) have not produced the decisive CT observables; therefore the true S1 character may remain underdetermined with current tool outputs.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Rotatable bonds exist, but there is no hallmark evidence of a twisted/dark CT lowest state (lowest state remains bright in vertical manifolds) and no excited-state relaxation evidence is available; thus TICT stays secondary.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No plausible intramolecular proton donor/acceptor pair for ESIPT.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Close-family external precedent for asymmetric methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens supports ICT/solvatochromism; internal CT metrics remain missing but the latest failure to quantify conformer torsional variation weakens the LE/neutral-aromatic surrogate argument.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Conformer-bundle vertical spectra show consistently bright lowest state with small excitation spread, compatible with an LE/neutral-aromatic manifold; however, inability to extract planarity/torsion descriptors from the conformer bundle limits interpretation of whether this robustness persists across meaningful twisting.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Key molecule-specific CT/state-character observables remain unavailable or tool-blocked (NTO/hole–electron/qCT; difference densities; orbital-descriptor keyword failures), and the conformer-geometry descriptor extraction also failed to return torsion/planarity metrics.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Rotatable bonds exist, but conformer-bundle evidence did not reveal a lowest-dark pattern across sampled conformers; without torsion/CT metrics or relaxed excited-state pathways, TICT remains secondary.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No ESIPT-capable intramolecular proton donor/acceptor pair is present (methoxy + nitrile only).

However, the intended interpretability step (extract torsion/planarity variation across conformers) failed due to missing geometry-descriptor outputs, so the surrogate LE/robustness argument cannot yet be anchored to meaningful geometric variation.
working_memory.capability_assessment: Verifier supplementation is already sufficient and high-information (close-family precedent + explicit solvatochromism/CT thresholds). Internally, direct CT descriptors remain unavailable (NTO/hole–electron/qCT; difference densities absent in baseline artifacts; LMO/NATORB keyword failures). The conformer-bundle route provides a viable surrogate discriminator, but the current parse-only geometry-descriptor extractor did not return torsion/planarity metrics; raw-bundle inspection is a low-risk way to locate geometry files/log outputs needed to compute torsion variation via an alternate supported pathway.
working_memory.stagnation_assessment: Mild stagnation risk: repeated attempts to obtain CT-defining observables have failed or returned proxy-only outputs, and the latest geometry-descriptor extraction also lacked key deliverables. However, the conformer bundle itself provided new internal evidence, and raw-artifact inspection is a concrete contraction aimed at unblocking torsion/planarity quantification without invoking unstable orbital-descriptor workflows.
working_memory.contraction_reason: The closure pair hinges on whether conformer robustness is observed across meaningful torsional variation. Since `extract_geometry_descriptors_from_bundle` did not surface torsion/planarity metrics, the best next step is to inspect the raw conformer-bundle artifacts to identify geometry/log files that can support torsion/planarity computation in a subsequent bounded, registry-supported parse/analysis step.
working_memory.information_gain_assessment: Medium: raw conformer-bundle artifact inspection can reveal hidden geometry/log outputs enabling torsion/planarity statistics, which would materially increase the discriminative power of the conformer-bundle robustness evidence for ICT vs neutral-aromatic.
working_memory.gap_trend: Partially narrowing via surrogate evidence (conformer-bundle robustness), but currently stalled on interpreting whether robustness spans meaningful torsional variation; next step targets that interpretability gap.

working_memory.main_gap: A molecule-specific, discriminative ICT-vs-neutral-aromatic test: does enforced torsional twisting introduce low-lying dark/CT-like states or strong state-ordering sensitivity (ICT/TICT-family), or does the lowest state remain bright/LE-like even at large twist (neutral aromatic/LE)?
working_memory.conflict_status: mixed_support: external close-family solvatochromism precedent favors ICT, while internal conformer-bundle evidence favors a robust bright/LE-like lowest state; direct CT metrics and torsion-variation quantification remain missing.
working_memory.planner_task_instruction: Microscopic: run exactly one registry-backed action `run_torsion_snapshots` for SMILES COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)c2ccccc2)c2ccccc2)cc1 to generate forced-twist geometries around the most relevant aryl–vinyl torsion(s) (preferably the donor anisole–vinyl linkage if auto-selected) and compute vertical excitations. Use an automatic dihedral selection policy if explicit atom indices are not required/available. Angle offsets: [0, 30, 60, 90] degrees. State window: [1, 3]. Deliverables: per-snapshot excitation energies and oscillator strengths plus a clear pattern summary indicating whether a low-lying dark/weak state emerges upon twisting (supporting ICT/TICT-family) or whether the lowest state remains bright/allowed across twists (supporting neutral aromatic/LE). Do not do excited-state relaxation.

working_memory.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT remains slightly favored mainly due to close-family solvatochromism-based precedent for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens, but internal molecule-specific CT descriptors remain unavailable/tool-blocked. Neutral-aromatic remains a near-tie because conformer-bundle vertical spectra show robust bright lowest-state behavior; however, we still cannot confirm that this robustness holds across meaningful torsional variation.
working_memory.capability_assessment: Verifier evidence is already sufficient and high-information for ICT vs neutral-aromatic at the close-family level (solvatochromism thresholds, donor–acceptor asymmetry criteria). Internally, direct CT metrics (NTO/hole–electron/qCT/difference density) are absent from baseline/conformer raw artifacts and some keyword-dependent routes have failed. A torsion-snapshot vertical-excitation route is a distinct, registry-supported way to create controlled torsional variation and test state-ordering/brightness sensitivity without relying on unavailable CT descriptor backends.
working_memory.stagnation_assessment: Some local stagnation exists in extracting torsion/planarity statistics from conformer artifacts (parse-only extraction did not yield torsion metrics). The proposed torsion-snapshot action changes the observable route (controlled twisting + vertical spectra) and should restore information gain for the ICT vs neutral-aromatic closure pair.
working_memory.contraction_reason: Because conformer-bundle parsing does not yield torsion/dihedral metrics and CT-defining descriptors remain unavailable, the most discriminative next step is to enforce twists explicitly (torsion snapshots) and observe whether low-lying dark/CT-like states emerge or whether bright S1 persists—directly separating ICT/TICT-family behavior from neutral-aromatic/LE robustness.
working_memory.information_gain_assessment: High expected information gain: torsion snapshots directly probe the geometry-sensitivity axis that distinguishes CT-involving mechanisms (often twist-sensitive) from LE/neutral-aromatic robustness, using outputs (energies/f-values/brightness patterns) that the current workflow reliably produces.
working_memory.gap_trend: Previously stalled on missing CT descriptors and missing torsion statistics; expected to narrow if torsion snapshots show (or refute) twist-induced emergence of low-lying dark/CT-like states.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Held slightly ahead due to sufficient close-family solvatochromism/ICT precedent; latest artifact inspection still does not provide molecule-specific CT metrics, so no internal strengthening.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Held close because conformer-bundle bright-S1 robustness remains LE-compatible; lack of torsion quantification prevents decisive upweighting.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted because neither conformer sampling nor current artifacts show emergence of low-lying dark states; still possible but unsupported.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Kept minimal: no structural ESIPT motif.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Kept moderate because key discriminators (CT metrics, torsion statistics) remain missing or unextracted, leaving residual ambiguity.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strong close-family precedent supports ICT for asymmetric methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens. New torsion-snapshot evidence shows the S1 oscillator strength collapses near 90° twist (f≈0.0006), consistent with charge-transfer-like/decoupled donor–acceptor behavior rather than a purely geometry-robust LE emitter.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Prior internal evidence (planarity proxy; bright S1 in baseline/conformer bundle) supported LE/neutral-aromatic competitiveness, but torsion snapshots show strong brightness quenching upon twisting, which is less consistent with a purely LE-dominated neutral-aromatic mechanism being robust to torsion.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Torsion snapshots reveal strong torsion sensitivity and near-dark S1 at 90° twist, which is qualitatively consistent with twisted/decoupled donor–acceptor configurations that can enable TICT-like behavior. Still lacks excited-state relaxation/minimum evidence, so remains below ICT.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Direct CT descriptors (NTO/hole–electron/qCT/difference density) remain unavailable/tool-blocked, and torsion-snapshot outputs appear to include only 2 excited states rather than the requested 1–3 window, leaving some residual ambiguity about state ordering and character.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton-transfer-capable donor/acceptor pair is present (methoxy + nitrile only).

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Second, independent aryl–vinyl torsion coordinate also shows strong twist-induced oscillator quenching and a lowest-state darkening with the first bright state shifting up ("lowest_state_is_dark_then_bright" at 90°). This repeatability across dihedrals supports a donor–acceptor decoupling / CT-mixed manifold consistent with ICT-based AIE behavior, and does not show clear energetic stabilization of the twisted dark state expected for TICT-dominated emission.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: At 90° twist the lowest vertical state becomes nearly dark (e.g., f(S1)≈0.0025) while a brighter state appears at higher energy (S2). This is compatible with a twisted CT-like configuration, but the dark S1 is not obviously stabilized (excitation energy does not drop vs 0–30°), and there is no excited-state relaxation/minimum evidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Earlier baseline/conformer-bundle evidence showed bright S1 and weak conformer sensitivity, but two different forced torsions now produce strong twist-induced darkening and state-ordering changes, which is difficult to reconcile with a purely LE/neutral-aromatic mechanism being dominant for this push–pull scaffold.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Direct CT-defining descriptors (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT/difference densities) remain unavailable/tool-blocked, so state character cannot be proven directly; remaining ambiguity is mainly ICT vs TICT subclassing rather than CT vs LE.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No intramolecular proton donor/acceptor motif exists (methoxy + nitrile only).

working_memory.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT is now supported by (i) close-family external precedent for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl emitters being ICT/solvatochromic and (ii) repeatable twist-induced darkening/state-ordering changes across two distinct aryl–vinyl torsions. Remaining uncertainty is intra-CT-family: whether emission is dominated by a planar/near-planar ICT state (ICT) or a stabilized twisted CT minimum (TICT), which cannot be proven without excited-state relaxation or a clear energetic stabilization trend from torsion coordinates.
working_memory.capability_assessment: Verifier evidence is already sufficient and high-information for ICT vs neutral-aromatic (close-family solvatochromism thresholds). Direct CT descriptors (NTO/hole–electron/qCT/difference density) remain unavailable/unstable in this environment, but torsion snapshots reliably provide a discriminative surrogate (state ordering/brightness vs enforced twist). Parse-only snapshot parsing is dependable and low-cost for completing per-angle state tables needed to discriminate ICT vs TICT.
working_memory.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: this round generated genuinely new discriminative evidence by probing a second torsion coordinate. The next step is a low-cost parse-only refinement to complete the evidence from the new bundle.
working_memory.contraction_reason: Complete ICT-vs-TICT closure using robust, already-working surrogate observables (state ordering and oscillator strengths vs enforced twist). Parsing the new torsion bundle is the minimal action to extract the missing per-angle multi-state details needed for that discrimination.
working_memory.information_gain_assessment: High: parsing the new torsion snapshot bundle should deliver the full per-angle state ordering/brightness needed to judge stabilization trends and sharpen ICT vs TICT.
working_memory.gap_trend: Narrowing: the main competition has contracted to ICT vs TICT, and the second torsion snapshot adds strong, repeatable twist-sensitivity evidence; remaining work is to quantify energetics/orderings across angles via parse-only extraction.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Upweighted: a second independent torsion coordinate reproduces strong twist-induced darkening and a bright-state shift to higher state index, consistent with donor–acceptor decoupling/CT-mixed manifolds and not showing clear energetic stabilization of the twisted dark state.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Slightly downweighted (relative to ICT): although a dark lowest state at 90° is TICT-compatible, the S1 excitation energy does not decrease with twist, weakening the case for a stabilized twisted CT minimum dominating emission.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Downweighted: repeated forced twisting produces near-dark S1 and state-ordering changes, inconsistent with a torsion-robust LE/neutral-aromatic mechanism as dominant.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Held: direct CT descriptors remain unavailable, but the torsion-snapshot surrogate is now strong enough that unstructured uncertainty is less necessary.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Unchanged minimal: no structural ESIPT motif.

working_memory.verifier_supplement_summary: No exact-identity literature hit; close-family evidence supports ICT for asymmetric methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens via positive solvatochromism/large Stokes shifts and provides explicit ICT-vs-LE discriminator thresholds; symmetric triarylethylenes without push–pull substitution show LE/neutral-aromatic emission with negligible solvatochromism.
working_memory.closure_justification_summary: Two distinct aryl–vinyl torsion coordinates both show strong twist sensitivity and (at ~90°) a dark S1 with brightness shifting to higher state index, supporting CT/decoupling behavior. Remaining closure is ICT vs TICT: whether twisting energetically stabilizes a low-lying dark CT-like state (TICT-like) versus mainly quenching oscillator strength without stabilization (ICT-like). Parsing the new torsion bundle across angles/states will quantify the energetic trend and state ordering needed for this discrimination.
working_memory.pairwise_task_rationale: A second controlled torsion coordinate (different aryl–vinyl dihedral) is the most discriminative surrogate to test ICT vs TICT when direct CT descriptors are unavailable; the follow-up parse step is needed to extract complete per-angle state tables from the new bundle.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Two independent aryl–vinyl torsion coordinates show repeatable twist-induced oscillator-strength collapse and state-ordering changes (at 90°: S1 nearly dark; first bright shifts to S2), consistent with donor–acceptor decoupling/CT-mixed manifolds expected for ICT-type AIEgens. In the newly parsed torsion set (dihedral [4,5,6,7]), the dark S1 is not energetically stabilized upon twisting (S1 energy rises from 4.228 eV at 0° to ~4.31–4.41 eV at 30–60° and is 4.365 eV at 90°), which argues against a stabilized twisted CT minimum dominating emission (i.e., against TICT as the primary label). Close-family literature precedent for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens also favors ICT/solvatochromism.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: At large twist (90°), the lowest vertical state becomes effectively dark (e.g., f≈0.0025 in the newly parsed torsion set) and a brighter state appears at higher energy (S2), which is qualitatively compatible with a twisted CT-like configuration. However, the key TICT hallmark—energetic stabilization of the twisted dark state—was not observed in the new per-angle energy trend (no decrease vs small-twist geometries), and there is still no excited-state relaxation/minimum evidence.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Earlier baseline/conformer-bundle evidence showed a bright lowest vertical excitation and weak conformer sensitivity, consistent with LE/neutral-aromatic emission, but controlled torsion snapshots across two dihedrals repeatedly show strong twist-induced darkening and brightness shifting to higher state index at 90°, which is difficult to reconcile with a torsion-robust purely LE mechanism dominating for this push–pull scaffold.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Direct CT-defining descriptors (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT/difference density) remain unavailable/blocked in this environment, so state character cannot be proven directly; residual uncertainty remains about the exact CT-vs-LE mixing and the role of twisted geometries beyond vertical snapshots.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No plausible intramolecular proton donor/acceptor pair exists (methoxy + nitrile only), and no evidence suggests ESIPT.

working_memory.verifier_supplement_summary: No exact-identity hit. Close-family push–pull cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens with alkoxy/methoxy donors and cyano acceptors are assigned ICT-AIE (single CT band; positive solvatochromism; RIR; no dual emission). Classic TICT hallmarks (dual emission; viscosity-dependent CT band; stabilized S1 minimum near ~90° twist) are emphasized as discriminators and are reported absent for close-family D–A triarylethylene/cyano-stilbene systems; computational studies place S1 minima at moderate twist, not 90°.
working_memory.closure_justification_summary: Internal: across two aryl–vinyl torsions, twisting causes oscillator-strength collapse and at 90° the lowest state becomes dark with brightness shifting to a higher state index, while the dark S1 is not energetically stabilized with twist. External: close-family literature assigns ICT-AIE (single CT band; no TICT dual emission; S1 minima at moderate twist) and provides mechanistic hallmarks that disfavor stabilized TICT minima for methoxy–cyano systems. Together these discriminate ICT over TICT sufficiently for closure.
working_memory.pairwise_task_rationale: Parsed per-angle state tables for the second torsion coordinate showed darkening with twist but no energetic stabilization of the dark S1, supporting ICT over TICT; combined with the targeted ICT-vs-TICT verifier supplement, this completes closure.

state_snapshot.latest_evidence_summary: Key internal: two independent aryl–vinyl torsion snapshot series show strong twist-induced oscillator quenching and, at 90°, a dark S1 with first bright shifting to S2; in the parsed second torsion set (atoms [4,5,6,7]) S1 energy does not decrease with twist (no stabilization). Key verifier: close-family D–A cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens are assigned ICT-AIE (single CT band; positive solvatochromism; RIR) and explicitly lack TICT hallmarks; TICT criteria emphasize dual emission, viscosity-dependent CT band, and stabilized S1 minimum near ~90° twist; methoxy donor is noted as generally weak for stabilized TICT.
state_snapshot.latest_main_gap: None remaining for mechanism labeling at the required granularity; remaining uncertainty is limited to uncomputed excited-state relaxation and unavailable direct CT descriptors, which do not overturn the ICT-over-TICT conclusion supported by both internal torsion trends and close-family external precedent.
state_snapshot.latest_conflict_status: resolved_toward_ICT: internal torsion-snapshot state-ordering/oscillator trends and external close-family precedent/hallmarks jointly favor ICT over TICT.
state_snapshot.latest_hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Residual uncertainty remains because direct CT descriptors (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT/difference density) and excited-state relaxation/minimum searches are not available in the internal toolchain; however, both (a) repeatable torsion-snapshot state-ordering/brightness behavior without energetic stabilization and (b) close-family external mechanistic precedent/hallmarks consistently disfavor TICT dominance for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triarylethylene scaffolds, making ICT the best-supported mechanism.
state_snapshot.latest_final_hypothesis_rationale: Assign ICT as the dominant emissive mechanism: internal controlled torsion snapshots show twist-induced decoupling (oscillator collapse; dark S1 at 90° with first bright shifting to higher state) without energetic stabilization of the twisted dark state, consistent with an ICT/CT-mixed emissive manifold whose emission is suppressed by rotation in solution and restored under restriction (AIE/RIR). Verifier close-family literature specifically reports ICT-AIE behavior (single CT band, positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts) and absence of TICT hallmarks (dual emission; stabilized ~90° S1 minimum), and notes methoxy donors are generally insufficient to yield stabilized TICT states. Together, these support ICT over TICT for this molecule.
state_snapshot.latest_capability_assessment: Internal microscopic workflows could not produce direct CT metrics (NTO/hole–electron/qCT/difference density) and some orbital-descriptor routes were unstable; however, torsion snapshots plus parse-only state tables provided a reliable surrogate discriminator (state ordering and oscillator strength vs enforced twist). Verifier retrieval provided close-family, mechanism-specific precedent and discriminator criteria for ICT vs TICT, supplying the missing external closure support.
state_snapshot.latest_stagnation_assessment: No stagnation at closure: the evidence chain shifted from missing CT descriptors to a robust surrogate (torsion snapshots) and then to targeted verifier supplementation for the remaining ICT-vs-TICT gap, yielding sufficient closure evidence within the remaining round budget.

last_planner_decision.diagnosis: Round-budget: current_round_index=19 of max_rounds=20 (rounds_remaining_including_current=2). Current top1=ICT (0.70) vs top2=TICT (0.15). hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal torsion snapshots across two independent aryl–vinyl dihedrals show repeatable twist-induced oscillator-strength collapse with state-ordering change at 90° (S1 becomes nearly dark; first bright shifts to S2), and S1 is not energetically stabilized upon twisting. Verifier close-family precedent for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens assigns emission to an ICT (single CT band) state with RIR/AIE, explicitly noting absence of TICT hallmarks and S1 minima at moderate twist (not ~90°).

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Twisting produces a very weakly allowed lowest state at 90° (dark S1) consistent with a twisted/decoupled CT-like configuration, but both internal torsion energetics (no stabilization of dark S1 with twist) and verifier precedent/hallmarks (no dual emission; no stabilized ~90° S1 minimum; methoxy donor typically insufficient for classic TICT) argue against TICT as the dominant emissive mechanism.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Earlier vertical/conformer evidence had bright S1 and weak conformer sensitivity, but controlled torsion forcing across two dihedrals robustly generates darkening and state-ordering changes, making a purely LE/neutral-aromatic dominant mechanism unlikely.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Direct CT-defining descriptors (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT/difference density) remain unavailable in the internal tool outputs, leaving some residual uncertainty about the precise CT/LE mixing and the role of relaxed excited-state minima.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No proton-transfer-capable donor/acceptor pair exists in the molecule and no evidence suggests ESIPT.

Overall, verifier evidence is strong enough to materially advance closure and supports ICT over TICT; closure justification for ICT vs TICT is now sufficient for decisive finalization.

decision.final_hypothesis_rationale: Assign ICT as the dominant emissive mechanism: internal controlled torsion snapshots show twist-induced decoupling (oscillator collapse; dark S1 at 90° with first bright shifting to higher state) without energetic stabilization of the twisted dark state, consistent with an ICT/CT-mixed emissive manifold whose emission is suppressed by rotation in solution and restored under restriction (AIE/RIR). Verifier close-family literature specifically reports ICT-AIE behavior (single CT band, positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts) and absence of TICT hallmarks (dual emission; stabilized ~90° S1 minimum), and notes methoxy donors are generally insufficient to yield stabilized TICT states. Together, these support ICT over TICT for this molecule.

raw_response.diagnosis: Task: assess the most likely AIE mechanism (ICT vs TICT vs ESIPT vs neutral-aromatic/RIM) for the given molecule (C22H17NO; triaryl substituted alkene with one methoxy donor and one nitrile acceptor). Current top1 is TICT (0.46) with runner-up neutral aromatic (0.28). TICT is a working hypothesis because the structure has moderate planarity but several rotatable bonds and a donor–acceptor axis that could enable a twisted CT minimum; however, the provided planarity proxy is high (~0.90), which could indicate a more rigid, near-planar conjugated system where emission changes in aggregates arise more from generic restriction of vibrations/rotations (neutral-aromatic/RIM) than from accessing a distinct TICT state. Evidence to separate TICT from neutral-aromatic/RIM: (i) whether S1 has strong CT character (dipole change, hole–electron separation) and whether twisting along a key dihedral is energetically favored in S1; (ii) whether a low-oscillator-strength CT/TICT state lies below a brighter LE-like state; (iii) whether S1 geometry relaxes toward a more twisted donor/aryl arrangement. Shared prepared structure context is available (neutral singlet; 10 conformers; selected conformer id 2; 4 rotatable bonds; prepared XYZ/SDF paths provided), enabling immediate low-cost baseline quantum calculations. Macro can provide structure-based mechanistic priors and identify likely torsional coordinates and D–A partitions but cannot confirm state ordering or CT character quantitatively. Microscopic can run a single bounded baseline S0 optimization plus S1 vertical excitation to extract excitation energy, oscillator strength, and qualitative CT indicators; it cannot in round-1 do conformer/torsion scans or multi-step excited-state relaxations. First round should gather both: macro to map plausible motion/CT pathways and microscopic baseline to anchor the hypothesis with electronic-structure observables.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run Macro (mechanistic priors from structure) and Microscopic (single bounded baseline S0/S1 calculation on the shared prepared structure) to discriminate TICT vs neutral-aromatic/RIM and assess ICT likelihood.

agent_task_instructions.Macro: Using the provided canonical SMILES and the shared-structure summary (rotatable bonds=4, planarity proxy~0.90, triaryl conjugation, methoxy donor, nitrile acceptor), provide: (1) likely AIE mechanism priors among ICT/TICT/neutral-aromatic; (2) identify the single most plausible torsional coordinate for TICT formation (describe the bond/fragment pair conceptually, e.g., donor-ring vs exocyclic C=C axis), and (3) predict qualitative signatures (solvatochromism, f drop, dipole increase) expected if TICT dominates. Do not make final selection; just provide discriminative expectations.
agent_task_instructions.Microscopic: Perform exactly one baseline-only S0/S1 bundle on the shared prepared structure (prepared_structure.xyz for conformer_id=2): aTB S0 geometry optimization (singlet, neutral) followed by a bounded S1 vertical excitation calculation (report lowest few states up to n=5 if standard for the bundle). Return: S0 optimized energy summary, S1 excitation energies, oscillator strengths, dominant transition character if available (e.g., HOMO->LUMO), and any CT indicators available in the baseline output (e.g., change in dipole or orbital localization narrative). No conformer/torsion sensitivity, no additional follow-ups.

Latest evidence adds (i) macro proxies: D–π–A motif with 4 rotatable bonds but high planarity_proxy≈0.899, and (ii) microscopic baseline: lowest vertical excitation is bright (S1 at 3.6776 eV, f=0.2876; pattern=lowest_state_is_bright) with HOMO–LUMO gap ~3.17 eV and sizable S0 dipole magnitude (~8.17 D). This weakens the prior Top-1 (TICT) relative to alternatives because a hallmark risk for TICT is formation of a dark twisted CT state; instead the lowest vertical state is already strongly allowed. It strengthens ICT and also keeps neutral-aromatic/LE competitive because high planarity and strong oscillator strength can indicate an LE-dominated transition. Current Top-1 is ICT (0.34) with Top-2 neutral aromatic (0.31); TICT drops to third (0.27). Key remaining discriminator between ICT vs neutral aromatic: whether S1 shows significant charge-transfer character (hole–electron separation / transferred charge / dipole change) versus being spatially overlapping LE on the conjugated triaryl-alkenyl core. Verifier supplementation remains missing; closure justification is still missing but can be advanced internally via a targeted CT-descriptor extraction from the already-generated baseline bundle. Therefore the best next action is a single bounded microscopic task to extract CT descriptors for the key low-lying bright state(s), directly discriminating ICT vs neutral aromatic (and secondarily TICT).

decision.diagnosis: Round budget: 1/20 (20 remaining). Latest evidence adds (i) macro proxies: D–π–A motif with 4 rotatable bonds but high planarity_proxy≈0.899, and (ii) microscopic baseline: lowest vertical excitation is bright (S1 at 3.6776 eV, f=0.2876; pattern=lowest_state_is_bright) with HOMO–LUMO gap ~3.17 eV and sizable S0 dipole magnitude (~8.17 D). This weakens the prior Top-1 (TICT) relative to alternatives because a hallmark risk for TICT is formation of a dark twisted CT state; instead the lowest vertical state is already strongly allowed. It strengthens ICT and also keeps neutral-aromatic/LE competitive because high planarity and strong oscillator strength can indicate an LE-dominated transition. Current Top-1 is ICT (0.34) with Top-2 neutral aromatic (0.31); TICT drops to third (0.27). Key remaining discriminator between ICT vs neutral aromatic: whether S1 shows significant charge-transfer character (hole–electron separation / transferred charge / dipole change) versus being spatially overlapping LE on the conjugated triaryl-alkenyl core. Verifier supplementation remains missing; closure justification is still missing but can be advanced internally via a targeted CT-descriptor extraction from the already-generated baseline bundle. Therefore the best next action is a single bounded microscopic task to extract CT descriptors for the key low-lying bright state(s), directly discriminating ICT vs neutral aromatic (and secondarily TICT).

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 3/20 (18 rounds remaining). Top1=neutral aromatic (0.38) vs Top2=ICT (0.30). Latest evidence weakly strengthens neutral aromatic relative to ICT: the targeted state-characterization run again failed to produce explicit CT observables (NTO/attachment–detachment/hole–electron separation/qCT all missing) and only returned dominant transitions and state-family overlap. The available proxy indicates S1 and S2 are in the same excitation family (shared occupied orbitals 81/82 and same primary virtual orbital 83; dominant transition signature reported as 82→83), which is more suggestive of a consistent delocalized π–π* manifold than a strongly state-specific CT signature. ICT remains a close challenger because the molecular topology is push–pull and the S0 dipole is large (~8.17 D), but without quantitative CT diagnostics we cannot distinguish 'delocalized LE with large permanent dipole' from genuine ICT. Key remaining discriminator (neutral aromatic vs ICT): spatial localization of frontier orbitals (HOMO vs LUMO) and/or quantitative CT metrics (charge separation/qCT), which are still unavailable. Verifier supplementation remains missing. Closure justification status remains collecting (the targeted task executed but did not yield the discriminative CT metrics). Next best action is another single bounded microscopic task that changes the observable acquisition route: compute localized-orbital / frontier-orbital localization descriptors to directly test donor-localized HOMO vs acceptor-localized LUMO (ICT) versus broadly delocalized frontier orbitals (neutral aromatic).
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run exactly one registry-backed action `run_targeted_localized_orbital_analysis` on the existing electronic-structure context (reuse artifact_bundle_id=`round_01_baseline_bundle`, fixed-geometry; no excited-state relaxation). Deliverables: localization/fragment-localization style summary for frontier orbitals (at minimum HOMO, HOMO-1, LUMO, LUMO+1 if available), indicating whether HOMO density is donor (anisole) biased and LUMO is acceptor (C#N/vinyl) biased (supporting ICT) vs both delocalized across the π-system (supporting neutral aromatic). Report any quantitative localization indices that Amesp provides; otherwise give a clear orbital-by-orbital qualitative assignment based on the computed localized-orbital output.

This leaves the neutral-aromatic vs ICT question unresolved: the key remaining discriminator is whether frontier orbitals/excitation density show donor→acceptor separation (supporting ICT) versus delocalized π-density (supporting neutral aromatic). The latest failure increases residual uncertainty (unknown upweighted) and indicates an internal route blockage for the intended closure task. Verifier supplementation is still missing, and closure justification remains collecting (internal targeted tasks have not produced the decisive CT/orbital metrics). Given the internal tool-path friction and the still-missing external check, the best next action is Verifier to obtain literature/precedent discriminators for closely related cyano-stilbene / triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens and any known ICT vs LE signatures (e.g., solvatochromism, Stokes shift, polarity dependence).
raw_response.task_instruction: Verifier: For the molecule with SMILES COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)c2ccccc2)c2ccccc2)cc1 (C22H17NO; methoxy-aryl + cyano-substituted triaryl-alkenyl scaffold), find external literature/precedent evidence that discriminates ICT vs neutral-aromatic (LE/RIM) emission. Focus specifically on: (1) whether analogous methoxy-cyano-stilbene / triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens are described as ICT/solvatochromic vs LE-dominated; (2) reported hallmarks: solvatochromism strength, large dipole change, large Stokes shift, emission broadening/red-shift with solvent polarity, aggregate-state blue/red shifts; (3) any explicit mechanistic assignment (ICT vs LE vs TICT) for close analogs (same cyano-vinyl-phenyl core with anisole donor). Provide 2–5 most relevant sources or well-established review statements, and summarize what evidence would favor ICT over neutral-aromatic for this scaffold.

Therefore: (i) ICT remains favored mainly by sufficient/high-gain external close-family precedent (solvatochromism-based ICT assignments for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl analogs), but is slightly weakened by the continued absence of a molecule-specific CT proxy; (ii) neutral aromatic is slightly strengthened as the main internal-model competitor because the internal evidence remains consistent with a delocalized LE-like manifold and the CT discriminator is still missing. Key remaining discriminator between ICT and neutral aromatic is a molecule-specific CT measure (e.g., transition-density spatial separation / attachment–detachment / fragment charge shift) or experimental solvatochromism magnitude for this exact compound. Verifier supplementation is sufficient; closure justification remains collecting (internal discriminator still missing). Given verifier is already sufficient, the best next step is a single bounded microscopic action that targets an alternative, registry-supported CT proxy not reliant on the previously failing LMO/NTO routes.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round budget: 7/20 (14 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.40) vs Top2=neutral aromatic (0.30). Latest evidence (microscopic targeted transition-dipole analysis on states 1–2) adds: (i) large S0 dipole magnitude (~8.1725 D), (ii) two bright low-lying transitions with sizable transition-dipole magnitudes (S0→S1 dip≈4.347; S0→S2 dip≈3.443) and oscillator strengths ~0.315 and ~0.294, and (iii) dominant transition structure (mainly 58→59 and related contributions). This strengthens the picture of an allowed, delocalized low-lying excitation but does not provide a charge-separation metric; therefore it does not decisively support ICT over neutral-aromatic. Net effect: ICT remains top1 mainly due to sufficient close-family verifier precedent; neutral-aromatic remains a close challenger because molecule-specific CT proxies are still missing. Key remaining discriminator ICT vs neutral-aromatic: a molecule-specific CT indicator for the emitting state (e.g., excited-state vs ground-state fragment charge shift, or NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT), or experimental solvatochromism magnitude for this exact compound. Verifier supplementation is sufficient; closure justification is still collecting because internal molecule-specific CT proxy remains absent. Next action should therefore generate an internal donor→acceptor charge-redistribution proxy (excited-state charge analysis) rather than more transition-dipole-only observables.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run exactly one registry-backed action `run_targeted_charge_analysis` reusing the existing baseline electronic-structure context (artifact_bundle_id=`round_01_baseline_bundle`, fixed-geometry; optimize_ground_state=false; no excited-state relaxation; no torsion scans). Target state_window=[1,2]. Deliverables: per-state atomic charges (and, if supported, state-minus-ground delta charges) suitable to assess donor (anisole/methoxy ring) → acceptor (vinyl-C#N region) charge redistribution. If only per-atom charges are available, also provide a compact summed charge over: (a) methoxy O + methyl + anisole ring atoms, and (b) nitrile N + nitrile C + adjacent vinyl carbon(s), for S0 vs S1 vs S2 (or best available proxy). Explicitly report what excited-state charge outputs are available vs missing.

normalized_response.main_gap: A molecule-specific ICT-vs-neutral-aromatic discriminator for S1/S2: NTO/hole–electron separation/overlap, qCT (or an equivalent CT metric), or at minimum an interpretable state-resolved donor→acceptor charge-redistribution proxy; alternatively, experimental solvatochromism magnitude for the exact compound.
normalized_response.conflict_status: mixed_but_ICT_tilt_from_external: verifier close-family precedent favors ICT, while internal planarity/bright-LE-compatible signatures keep neutral-aromatic plausible; internal CT metrics remain missing and now appear tool-blocked.
normalized_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT remains the best-supported label primarily from strong close-family external precedent (solvatochromism-based ICT assignments for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens). However, repeated inability to obtain molecule-specific CT descriptors (and now explicit backend keyword failures for both LMO and NATORB routes) keeps neutral-aromatic as a near-tie challenger and increases the probability that the mechanism cannot be decisively confirmed with current internal descriptor tooling.
normalized_response.capability_assessment: Verifier supplementation is already strong/sufficient at the close-family level. Internally, several CT/state-character routes are either unavailable (proxy-only) or unstable (keyword errors: "lmo", now "natorb"), which blocks the intended NTO/qCT-based closure. A pragmatic next step is to inspect raw artifact contents to locate any existing but unparsed CT-relevant outputs, rather than repeatedly triggering failing descriptor-generation workflows.
normalized_response.stagnation_assessment: Internal closure is trending toward stagnation on the same missing discriminator: molecule-specific CT metrics. The latest round adds a second clear backend keyword failure (natorb), suggesting a systematic limitation of these descriptor routes in the current environment. Switching to artifact inspection is a contraction to a lower-risk step that can unblock a new parse-only path.
normalized_response.contraction_reason: Natural-orbital and localized-orbital descriptor routes are currently unstable (keyword errors) and multiple other targeted analyses have returned proxy-only outputs. normalized_response.information_gain_assessment: Medium: raw artifact inspection can reveal hidden/parsable CT-relevant outputs and may unlock a decisive parse-only discriminator without relying on failing descriptor-generation keywords.
normalized_response.gap_trend: Internally stalled on obtaining CT descriptors (now with repeated backend keyword failures); external constraint remains strong for ICT. Artifact inspection is intended to restart internal progress by identifying extractable CT proxies already present.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 11/20 (10 rounds remaining). Current top1=ICT (0.33) vs top2=neutral aromatic (0.30). Latest evidence (microscopic inspect_raw_artifact_bundle on round_01_baseline_bundle) weakens internal support for ICT relative to neutral aromatic by confirming that the baseline bundle lacks essentially all molecule-specific CT discriminators: no NTO/natorb outputs, no attachment–detachment, no transition-density/difference-density files, and no per-state charge dumps. What is present is limited to vertical energies/f-values, dominant transitions, S0 dipole, MO files, and logs—insufficient to decisively label S1 as CT vs LE. This leaves the external close-family solvatochromism precedent as the main reason ICT remains top1, while neutral aromatic stays highly competitive because internal outputs are consistent with an LE-like manifold and cannot be disproven. Key remaining discriminator for ICT vs neutral aromatic: a molecule-specific CT metric (hole–electron separation/qCT/difference density) OR a robust surrogate such as consistent darkening/character change across conformers indicative of CT stabilization. Verifier supplementation remains sufficient (close-family + discriminator thresholds), but closure justification is still collecting because internal molecule-specific CT evidence is missing and now confirmed absent in baseline artifacts. Best next action is to generate a richer internal dataset via a different bounded calculation route (multi-conformer vertical excitations) to look for state-ordering/brightness patterns across conformers and potentially produce additional parsable artifacts.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run exactly one registry-backed action `run_conformer_bundle` for SMILES COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)c2ccccc2)c2ccccc2)cc1 to compute vertical excited-state manifolds across multiple low-energy conformers (use the existing shared structure context; do not do excited-state relaxation). Deliverables: for each conformer, report S1–S3 energies and oscillator strengths and whether the lowest state is bright vs dark. Goal: find whether state ordering/brightness is robustly LE-like (always bright lowest state) or whether some conformers show a low-lying dark/weakly allowed state consistent with CT mixing that could support ICT over neutral-aromatic.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute ONE Amesp registry action: `run_conformer_bundle`. Use the shared prepared structure context if available. Constraints: no excited-state relaxation; keep bounded (e.g., max_conformers=5–10 if parameterized by the action defaults). Report per-conformer vertical excited-state summary for states 1–3: energies (eV), oscillator strengths, and the pattern 'lowest_state_is_bright' vs 'lowest_state_is_dark' (or closest available ordering summary). Do not attempt global mechanism selection.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 14/20 (7 rounds remaining). Current top1=ICT (0.34) vs top2=neutral aromatic (0.33). Latest evidence (microscopic inspect_raw_artifact_bundle on round_12_conformer_bundle) is low-discriminative for ICT vs neutral aromatic: it confirms the conformer bundle’s raw artifacts mostly comprise prepared geometry paths plus aTB/TD outputs (AIP/AOP/MO/stdout) and do not expose explicit dihedral/torsion tables or per-conformer torsion metrics in a parser-friendly way. This leaves the key surrogate argument ("bright S1 is robust even across meaningfully twisted conformers") still under-justified because torsional spread cannot be quantified. Consequently: (i) ICT is not strengthened by new molecule-specific CT observables (still missing), but retains support from sufficient close-family solvatochromism-based precedent; (ii) neutral aromatic remains strongly competitive due to conformer-robust bright lowest-state behavior, but is not decisively advantaged without demonstrated torsional diversity. Key remaining discriminator for ICT vs neutral aromatic: whether twisting (forced torsion) induces CT-like/dark-state behavior or strong state-ordering sensitivity (supporting ICT/TICT-family) versus persistent bright/LE-like lowest-state behavior even at large twists (supporting neutral aromatic/LE). Verifier supplementation remains sufficient; closure justification remains collecting because internal, molecule-specific CT descriptors are unavailable and surrogate geometry-variation quantification is blocked. Best next action is a bounded torsion-snapshot calculation that deliberately enforces twist angles on a high-relevance aryl–vinyl torsion and checks state ordering/brightness changes, which directly targets ICT vs neutral aromatic without requiring unavailable NTO/qCT tooling.

decision.information_gain_assessment: High expected information gain: torsion snapshots directly probe the geometry-sensitivity axis that distinguishes CT-involving mechanisms (often twist-sensitive) from LE/neutral-aromatic robustness, using outputs (energies/f-values/brightness patterns) that the current workflow reliably produces.
decision.gap_trend: Previously stalled on missing CT descriptors and missing torsion statistics; expected to narrow if torsion snapshots show (or refute) twist-induced emergence of low-lying dark/CT-like states.

hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ICT: Held slightly ahead due to sufficient close-family solvatochromism/ICT precedent; latest artifact inspection still does not provide molecule-specific CT metrics, so no internal strengthening.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.neutral aromatic: Held close because conformer-bundle bright-S1 robustness remains LE-compatible; lack of torsion quantification prevents decisive upweighting.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.TICT: Downweighted because neither conformer sampling nor current artifacts show emergence of low-lying dark states; still possible but unsupported.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.ESIPT: Kept minimal: no structural ESIPT motif.
hypothesis_reweight_explanation.unknown: Kept moderate because key discriminators (CT metrics, torsion statistics) remain missing or unextracted, leaving residual ambiguity.

decision.verifier_supplement_summary: No exact-identity literature hit; close-family evidence supports ICT for asymmetric methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens via positive solvatochromism/large Stokes shifts and provides explicit ICT-vs-LE discriminator thresholds; symmetric triarylethylenes without push–pull substitution show LE/neutral-aromatic emission with negligible solvatochromism.
decision.closure_justification_summary: Current closure rests on surrogate internal evidence (conformer-robust bright S1) versus strong external ICT precedent. Because torsion/planarity variation could not be extracted from the conformer bundle, the surrogate evidence is not fully discriminative. Next step enforces twisting via torsion snapshots and evaluates whether low-lying dark/CT-like states emerge (favoring ICT/TICT-family) or whether bright S1 persists (favoring neutral aromatic/LE).
decision.pairwise_task_rationale: Raw conformer-bundle inspection was needed to verify whether torsion/dihedral outputs existed for parse-only geometry statistics; inspection indicates torsion/dihedral reporting is not available, motivating a controlled torsion-snapshot calculation as the next discriminative surrogate.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Strong close-family external precedent for asymmetric methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens being ICT/solvatochromic remains the main support; internal CT-defining descriptors are still unavailable, so this is still prior-weighted rather than molecule-specifically proven.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Internal surrogate evidence remains supportive: conformer-bundle vertical spectra show the lowest state is consistently bright with very small excitation-energy spread across conformers, consistent with an LE/neutral-aromatic manifold; however, torsion/planarity variation across conformers still cannot be quantified with available parsed outputs.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Repeated inability to obtain CT-defining observables (NTO/hole–electron/qCT/difference density) plus missing torsion/dihedral reporting from conformer artifacts keeps a substantial chance that the emissive-state character cannot be resolved with current tool outputs.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Geometric freedom exists, but conformer sampling did not reveal any conformer where the lowest state becomes dark/weakly allowed; without explicit twisting-induced state reordering evidence, TICT remains secondary.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: No plausible intramolecular proton-transfer motif (no phenolic OH / H-bonded donor–acceptor pair).

hypothesis_pool.rationale: Prior internal evidence (planarity proxy; bright S1 in baseline/conformer bundle) supported LE/neutral-aromatic competitiveness, but torsion snapshots show strong brightness quenching upon twisting, which is less consistent with a purely LE-dominated neutral-aromatic mechanism being robust to torsion.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT

Still lacks excited-state relaxation/minimum evidence, so remains below ICT.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Direct CT descriptors (NTO/hole–electron/qCT/difference density) remain unavailable/tool-blocked, and torsion-snapshot outputs appear to include only 2 excited states rather than the requested 1–3 window, leaving some residual ambiguity about state ordering and character.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round budget: 15/20 (6 rounds remaining). Current top1=ICT (0.42) vs top2=neutral aromatic (0.25); third=TICT (0.18). Latest evidence (torsion snapshots, 0/30/60/90° on an auto-selected high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral) strengthens ICT-family interpretations and challenges a purely neutral-aromatic/LE picture: the lowest excitation remains nominally the lowest bright state at small twists (f≈0.10 at 0–30°) but becomes effectively dark at 90° (f≈0.0006) with an excitation-energy increase (~4.40→4.66 eV) and substantial oscillator-spread (~0.10). This ‘bright-to-dark upon decoupling’ is a key CT/twist-sensitivity hallmark (supporting ICT/TICT-family) and is less consistent with an LE/neutral-aromatic state remaining allowed across torsion. The remaining discriminator between ICT vs TICT is whether emission is dominated by a planar/partially planar ICT state (ICT) versus a relaxed twisted CT minimum (TICT), which cannot be established without excited-state relaxation or robust state-character descriptors. Verifier supplementation remains sufficient (close-family solvatochromism-based ICT precedent), while closure justification is still collecting because the torsion run did not fully return the requested state window (only 2 states reported) and lacks state-character metrics.
raw_response.task_instruction: Microscopic: run exactly one registry-backed action `parse_snapshot_outputs` on artifact_bundle_id=`round_15_torsion_snapshots` to extract per-snapshot vertical excited-state manifolds with clear state indexing. Deliverables: for each snapshot angle (0, 30, 60, 90°) report all available excited states with excitation_energy_ev and oscillator_strength, and explicitly indicate whether any low-lying (within the computed manifold) dark/weakly allowed state emerges as torsion approaches 90°. Also report whether the state_count=2 limitation is real (i.e., only two states were computed) or a parsing/subset artifact.

agent_task_instructions.microscopic: Execute ONE Amesp action: `parse_snapshot_outputs` using artifact_bundle_id=`round_15_torsion_snapshots` (parse-only; no new calculations). Output per-snapshot (torsion_01..04) excited-state tables (state_index, excitation_energy_ev, oscillator_strength, dominant_transitions if present) and a note on whether missing state(s) in the requested window [1,3] were not computed vs not parsed.

raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: ICT is now favored by (i) strong close-family solvatochromism-based precedent and (ii) new twist-induced quenching (S1 becomes nearly dark at 90°), consistent with donor–acceptor decoupling/CT-like behavior. However, the system still lacks direct CT descriptors (qCT/hole–electron/NTO) and excited-state relaxation evidence, so the split between ICT vs TICT (and residual LE mixing in the emissive state) remains uncertain.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Verifier evidence is already sufficient and high-information (close-family solvatochromism thresholds and ICT-vs-LE discriminators). Internally, direct CT descriptors remain unavailable/tool-blocked, but torsion snapshots successfully provide a robust surrogate discriminator (twist sensitivity of oscillator strength). The immediate capability need is reliable parsing/complete reporting of the torsion-snapshot excited-state manifold across angles to avoid misinterpreting an incomplete state window.
raw_response.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation: this round produced genuinely discriminative new internal evidence (torsion-induced bright→dark behavior) that directly targets the ICT vs neutral-aromatic closure pair.
raw_response.contraction_reason: Parse-only post-processing of the existing torsion-snapshot bundle is the lowest-cost step to (a) confirm state ordering across angles and (b) verify whether the limited state_count is a computation limit or a parsing/reporting artifact, before scheduling any further new calculations.
raw_response.evidence_summary: Latest microscopic torsion snapshots (auto-selected aryl–vinyl dihedral dih_0_1_2_3; angles 0/30/60/90°; fixed-geometry singlepoints) show: S1 excitation energy increases from 4.4007 eV (0°) to 4.6561 eV (90°) while oscillator strength drops from 0.103 (0°) to 0.0006 (90°). Reported torsion sensitivity: excitation_spread_ev=0.2554, oscillator_spread=0.1024, torsion_sensitive=true. Lowest-state brightness transitions from allowed to essentially dark at high twist.
raw_response.main_gap: Finalize a molecule-specific ICT vs neutral-aromatic decision using the torsion-snapshot surrogate cleanly: confirm full per-angle state ordering/brightness (including any missing state(s) in the requested [1,3] window) and interpret whether twist-induced darkening reflects CT/decoupling (supports ICT/TICT-family) versus a robust LE manifold (supports neutral aromatic).
raw_response.conflict_status: mixed_but_now_tilting_ICT: external close-family solvatochromism precedent favors ICT; new internal torsion snapshots show strong twist-induced oscillator quenching (CT/decoupling-like), reducing support for neutral-aromatic LE robustness.
raw_response.information_gain_assessment: High: torsion snapshots introduced a direct, discriminative twist-sensitivity observable (bright→dark at 90°). Parsing the snapshot outputs should complete the per-angle state-ordering picture at minimal cost.
raw_response.gap_trend: Narrowing: surrogate discriminators (torsion sensitivity) are now directly informative for ICT vs neutral-aromatic; remaining uncertainty is mostly about completeness/interpretation of the state manifold and ICT vs TICT subclassing.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round 16/20 (5 rounds remaining). Top1=ICT (0.45) and top2=TICT (0.23); neutral aromatic drops to third (0.18). Latest microscopic evidence (parsed torsion snapshot outputs for 0/30/60/90°) strengthens the CT/twist-sensitivity picture: at 90° the lowest state becomes essentially dark (S1 f=0.0006) and the first bright state shifts to S2 (4.921 eV, f=0.152), whereas at 0–60° the lowest state remains bright (S1 f≈0.103→0.087). This behavior challenges a torsion-robust neutral-aromatic LE assignment and instead supports ICT/TICT-family mechanisms. The key remaining discriminator is ICT vs TICT: does emission originate from a (partially) planar CT-mixed bright state (ICT) or from a relaxed twisted CT minimum (TICT). Verifier supplementation is sufficient for the earlier ICT-vs-neutral-aromatic question but is not specific for ICT-vs-TICT here; closure justification for ICT-vs-TICT is still collecting. The best next action is to run a second controlled torsion snapshot on a different high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral to see whether any twist coordinate stabilizes a low-lying dark state (more TICT-like) or mainly causes decoupling/brightness loss without stabilization (more ICT-like).

Deliverables: per-angle excitation energies and oscillator strengths plus state-ordering pattern (whether lowest becomes dark and whether a bright state becomes S2/S3). Goal: discriminate ICT vs TICT by testing whether any torsion coordinate produces a stabilized low-lying dark state (TICT-like) versus mainly oscillator quenching without energetic stabilization (ICT-like decoupling).

Angle_offsets_deg=[0,30,60,90]. state_window=[1,2,3]. Constraints: optimize_ground_state=false; no excited-state relaxation; bounded snapshot_count=4. raw_response.diagnosis:
Round 17/20 (rounds remaining incl. current: 4). Top1=ICT (0.52) vs Top2=TICT (0.20); additional candidates: neutral aromatic (0.13), unknown (0.13), ESIPT (0.02).

Latest evidence (microscopic run_torsion_snapshots on a DIFFERENT high-relevance aryl–vinyl dihedral, dihedral_id=dih_4_5_6_7) strengthens the CT/twist-decoupling picture and thereby strengthens top1 ICT relative to top2 TICT: across angle offsets 0→30→60→90°, the S1 oscillator strength collapses (f: 0.3302 → 0.2966 → 0.1557 → 0.0025) and at 90° the manifold is explicitly "lowest_state_is_dark_then_bright" with the first bright state being S2 (E≈5.4288 eV, f≈0.2018). This repeats the key qualitative behavior previously seen for the first torsion coordinate: twist drives the lowest state dark and shifts brightness upward in state index.

Effect on top2 (TICT): the 90°-twisted geometry producing a very dark S1 is TICT-compatible, but the excitation energy of S1 does not show a clear stabilization upon twisting (e.g., S1 energy at 90° ≈4.3647 eV vs 0° ≈4.2281 eV), suggesting twist mainly quenches oscillator strength via decoupling rather than stabilizing a low-lying twisted CT minimum (a more TICT-specific hallmark).

Effect on an additional candidate (neutral aromatic): two independent torsions now show strong twist-induced darkening/state-ordering change, which further disfavors a torsion-robust LE/neutral-aromatic mechanism.

Key remaining discriminator for ICT vs TICT: whether any torsional coordinate yields energetic stabilization (lowering) of a twisted, dark CT-like state indicative of a TICT minimum (or at least a systematically lowering S1 energy with twist), versus twist acting mainly as an oscillator-quenching coordinate without stabilizing a lower-energy twisted emissive state.

Verifier supplementation: already sufficient for ICT-vs-neutral-aromatic (close-family solvatochromism/ICT precedent); not the limiting factor now.
Closure justification: still collecting for ICT vs TICT; the best next step is to fully parse the new torsion-snapshot per-angle state tables (states 1–3) to quantify state ordering/brightness trends across the second dihedral and compare energetics more cleanly.

Why the chosen next action is best now: parse-only extraction from the newly generated torsion snapshot bundle is the lowest-cost way to obtain complete per-angle state tables (energies, oscillator strengths, dominant transitions if present) for this second torsion coordinate, directly targeting the ICT-vs-TICT discriminator without invoking previously unstable CT-descriptor workflows.

Goal: quantify whether the lowest dark state is energetically stabilized upon twisting (supporting TICT) or mainly oscillator-quenched without stabilization (supporting ICT).

decision.diagnosis:
Round 18/20 (rounds remaining incl. current: 3). Top1=ICT (0.58) vs Top2=TICT (0.22); additional candidates: neutral aromatic (0.10), unknown (0.08), ESIPT (0.02).

Latest evidence (microscopic parse_snapshot_outputs of round_17_torsion_snapshots; dihedral atoms [4,5,6,7]) strengthens ICT relative to TICT by providing complete per-angle state tables showing: (i) strong twist-induced oscillator quenching of S1 (f: 0° 0.3302 → 30° 0.2966 → 60° 0.1557 → 90° 0.0025), (ii) at 90° the first bright state shifts to S2 (pattern=lowest_state_is_dark_then_bright), and (iii) no clear energetic stabilization of the dark S1 upon twisting (S1 energies: 0° 4.2281 eV; 30° 4.3101 eV; 60° 4.4085 eV; 90° 4.3647 eV). This pattern is consistent with donor–acceptor decoupling that primarily removes oscillator strength and alters state ordering, rather than producing a distinctly stabilized twisted CT minimum.

Effect on top2 (TICT): still plausible because a dark lowest state appears at 90°, but weakened because the dark state is not lowered in energy vs near-planar geometries (no stabilization trend), and evidence remains vertical-only (no relaxed excited-state minimum).

Effect on an additional candidate (neutral aromatic): further weakened because robust LE-like behavior would not typically produce repeated lowest-state darkening and bright-state index shifts under enforced aryl–vinyl twisting.

Key remaining discriminator (ICT vs TICT): whether a twisted CT-like geometry is energetically favored on S1 (a stabilized twisted minimum) versus being mainly a dark, higher-energy vertical configuration reached only by forcing torsion. External precedent specifically about ICT vs TICT assignments in close analogs would be highly discriminative.

Verifier supplementation: sufficient for ICT vs neutral aromatic, but missing for ICT vs TICT. Closure justification: collecting (internal evidence is strong; external ICT-vs-TICT precedent still missing). Next action chooses verifier because it can directly address the remaining top1-vs-top2 mechanism split with external discriminator criteria for this scaffold.
decision.task_instruction: Verifier: For the molecule COc1ccc(/C(=C(/C#N)c2ccccc2)c2ccccc2)cc1 (methoxy donor + cyano acceptor triaryl-alkenyl/cyanovinyl scaffold), retrieve external literature/precedent that discriminates ICT vs TICT as the dominant emissive mechanism in close analogs. Focus on: (1) whether analogous methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triaryl-alkenyl AIEgens are assigned planar ICT (solvatochromic but emissive from a relatively planar ICT/LE-mixed state) vs TICT (emissive from a stabilized twisted CT minimum); (2) reported hallmarks that separate ICT vs TICT (e.g., viscosity dependence vs polarity dependence, temperature effects, dual emission, twist-induced dark state stabilization, excited-state minimum discussions, computational evidence of S1 minimum at ~90°); (3) any explicit statements about cyano-stilbene/triarylethylene push–pull systems having (or not having) a stabilized TICT minimum. Provide 2–5 specific sources or strong review statements and summarize the discriminator signals that would favor ICT over TICT for this scaffold.

raw_response.diagnosis: Round-budget: current_round_index=19 of max_rounds=20 (rounds_remaining_including_current=2). Current top1=ICT (0.70) vs top2=TICT (0.15). Verifier supplementation for ICT__vs__TICT is now sufficient and close-family specific: (i) close-family D–A cyanovinyl/triarylethylene AIEgens are consistently assigned ICT (single CT band, positive solvatochromism, RIR/AIE) rather than a stabilized dark TICT minimum; (ii) classic TICT hallmarks require dual emission, viscosity-dependent CT band formation, and an energetically stabilized S1 minimum near ~90° twist; methoxy donors are cited as generally insufficient for stabilized TICT compared with dimethylamino donors; (iii) close-family mechanistic studies report S1 minima at moderate twist (not 90°) and explicitly note no TICT dual-band signature. This external evidence aligns with internal torsion-snapshot results: twisting strongly quenches oscillator strength and shifts the first bright state upward, but the dark S1 is not energetically stabilized with twist (argues against TICT dominance). Overall, verifier evidence is strong enough to materially advance closure and supports ICT over TICT; closure justification for ICT vs TICT is now sufficient for decisive finalization.
raw_response.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Residual uncertainty remains because direct CT descriptors (NTO/hole–electron separation/qCT/difference density) and excited-state relaxation/minimum searches are not available in the internal toolchain; however, both (a) repeatable torsion-snapshot state-ordering/brightness behavior without energetic stabilization and (b) close-family external mechanistic precedent/hallmarks consistently disfavor TICT dominance for methoxy–cyano cyanovinyl/triarylethylene scaffolds, making ICT the best-supported mechanism.
raw_response.final_hypothesis_rationale: Assign ICT as the dominant emissive mechanism: internal controlled torsion snapshots show twist-induced decoupling (oscillator collapse; dark S1 at 90° with first bright shifting to higher state) without energetic stabilization of the twisted dark state, consistent with an ICT/CT-mixed emissive manifold whose emission is suppressed by rotation in solution and restored under restriction (AIE/RIR). Verifier close-family literature specifically reports ICT-AIE behavior (single CT band, positive solvatochromism, large Stokes shifts) and absence of TICT hallmarks (dual emission; stabilized ~90° S1 minimum), and notes methoxy donors are generally insufficient to yield stabilized TICT states. Together, these support ICT over TICT for this molecule.
raw_response.capability_assessment: Internal microscopic workflows could not produce direct CT metrics (NTO/hole–electron/qCT/difference density) and some orbital-descriptor routes were unstable; however, torsion snapshots plus parse-only state tables provided a reliable surrogate discriminator (state ordering and oscillator strength vs enforced twist). Verifier retrieval provided close-family, mechanism-specific precedent and discriminator criteria for ICT vs TICT, supplying the missing external closure support.
raw_response.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation at closure: the evidence chain shifted from missing CT descriptors to a robust surrogate (torsion snapshots) and then to targeted verifier supplementation for the remaining ICT-vs-TICT gap, yielding sufficient closure evidence within the remaining round budget.
raw_response.contraction_reason: Finalize because (i) verifier supplementation is sufficient for ICT__vs__TICT, and (ii) closure justification is now sufficient: internal torsion energetics/brightness trends and external close-family mechanistic hallmarks jointly discriminate ICT over TICT.
